title: tethys: track parent_symbol_id for nested symbols
description: When indexing symbols, parent_symbol_id is always set to None. Implement proper parent symbol tracking so nested symbols (e.g., methods inside structs/impls) have their parent relationship recorded.
notes: Closed: Duplicate of tethys-aay4 (same parent_symbol_id-always-None gap; tethys-fe4f was previously closed as a dup of aay4).

title: C# parser does not extract member declarations (properties, fields, events, delegates): [Obsolete] on them is invisible
description:
Found by the tethys-haw5 prove-it probe against a real C# repo (Tethys.Results).

C# member-level declarations are not extracted as symbols: properties, fields, events, and delegate types. Consequence: [Obsolete] on any of them is invisible to deprecated-callers (no symbols row to attach the attribute to), and member READS (member_access_expression, e.g. `result.Data`) produce no refs rows — extract_references handles only invocation_expression and object_creation_expression.

Original evidence (properties): `public T Data => Value;` (Tethys.Results GenericResult.cs:38) with [Obsolete] produced no symbol, no attribute, no refs. The repo's ONLY [Obsolete] sits on that property — method-scoped tethys-haw5 reports zero findings on such repos. Scope extended to fields/events/delegates during haw5 falsifiable-design: they share the same extraction gap.

Evidence: .tethys-haw5/probe1.sh + findings.md in the tethys repo.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] property_declaration extracted as a symbol kind (incl. expression-bodied)
- [ ] member_access_expression reads produce refs rows resolvable under existing conservative corroboration rules
- [ ] [Obsolete] property surfaces in deprecated-callers with its reader sites
notes: Closed: Shipped: PR #20 merged to main (dbb1a83). All 3 ACs fenced: property symbols incl. expression-bodied (extracts_property_* unit family, corpus 42/42 vs independent probe walk); member_access_expression reads as field_access refs under existing conservative rules (member_read_* family, member_read_cross_file_stays_qualified, corpus 881/881); [Obsolete] property surfaces in deprecated-callers with reader sites (csharp_obsolete_property_reader_sites with embedded decoy — corpus: Data property with exactly its 2 genuine Maybe sites). Build-time amendment D10 (user-approved at stop-on-drift): data-member symbols get their own Pass-1 map + Call/Construct kind gate after the corpus audit caught 2 fabricated call edges; call_edges and call/construct refs bit-identical to pre-feature baselines. Fixed in-branch: RUSTSEC-2026-0204 lockfile bump (crossbeam-epoch 0.9.20, was failing Cargo Deny repo-wide). Filed: tethys-5uqz (remaining read shapes), tethys-0aqj (kind-blind binding). Artifacts: .tethys-xebx/ (probes, design, plan, audit, baselines).

notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-rddn (BatchWriter error-handling bundle); the three facets share one design decision and should ship as one PR.

title: tethys: streaming compute_all_dependencies re-inserts file_deps from orphan files (deleted-from-disk but still in DB)
description:
Streaming-mode indexing (IndexOptions::with_streaming()) calls compute_all_dependencies (crates/tethys/src/indexing.rs:943) which iterates self.db.list_all_files() — the FULL set of files in the DB, including files that have been deleted from disk since their last index. For each such orphan, it loads the stale stored imports + refs and calls compute_dependencies_from_stored, which re-inserts file_deps rows with the orphan''s file_id as from_file_id.

Downstream queries (coupling Ce/Ca, callers, cycles, impact analysis) then see the orphan as a real source of cross-file edges, producing phantom contributions.

**Asymmetries — which paths are affected:**

| Path | Status |
|---|---|
| tethys index --rebuild (any mode) | Not affected — db.reset() wipes DB; orphans gone |
| tethys index non-streaming (default) | Not affected — compute_dependencies runs per disk-file in the parse loop; orphans never seen |
| tethys index streaming | **Affected** |

**Root cause:** index_with_options has no orphan-cleanup pass. db/files.rs:145-146 only DELETEs symbols/imports when an EXISTING file is RE-indexed (still on disk). Files deleted from disk are never processed, so no DELETE fires for them. FileChange::Deleted is detected in reindex.rs:122 but only by get_stale_files() (observation API, not called from indexing).

**How discovered:** Gemini code review on PR #65 flagged "compute_all_dependencies may re-calculate dependencies for stale entries (files in DB but deleted from disk)." Initial round-1 verdict was "reject (not a real bug)" on cascade-cleanup grounds; pressure-test revealed the cascade only applies to re-indexed files, not orphans. See .rivets-lcb6/review-decisions-round-1.md for the corrected verdict.

**Not caused by rivets-lcb6.** Pre-existing in streaming mode. rivets-lcb6''s clear_all_file_deps even partially mitigates by wiping file_deps each run, but compute_all_dependencies immediately re-inserts from orphans.

**Likely fix shape:** before compute_all_dependencies runs, delete file rows whose disk path doesn''t resolve (and let FK cascades clean up dependents). Either:
1. New cleanup_orphan_files that classifies via the same staleness check as reindex.rs::classify_indexed_file and DELETEs orphan rows, called from index_with_options before resolver/dep passes.
2. Filter compute_all_dependencies to skip files whose path doesn''t exist on disk (lighter touch, leaves orphan rows in files table).

Option 1 is cleaner — once orphan files are gone from the DB, all downstream queries are correct without per-callsite filtering.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Regression test: index a 2-file workspace in streaming mode, delete one file from disk, re-index (no --rebuild). Verify (a) the orphan no longer appears in list_all_files, OR (b) file_deps contains no rows with from_file_id == orphan's id.
- [ ] Equivalent test for non-streaming mode confirms the bug doesn't manifest there (regression fence).
- [ ] No perf regression: indexing a workspace with N files and 0 orphans takes the same time before and after.
notes: Closed: Fixed: PR #25 (merge bcc5138, fix 852317a). Orphan-cleanup pass purge_orphan_files in index_with_options confirms deleted-from-disk via classify_indexed_file before Index::delete_files (single transaction, FK cascades). Repro/fence: tests/orphan_files.rs (reindex_after_disk_delete_leaves_no_file_deps_from_orphan, reindex_after_disk_delete_purges_orphan_rows, reindex_without_deletion_keeps_all_file_rows; each batch+streaming) + delete_files_cascades_to_all_dependent_tables. Mechanism narrower than filed: needs an orphan ref surviving with reference_name intact (resolution nulls it) — real for macro-generated/cfg-gated targets. Also killed an unfiled second path: orphan-to-orphan resolved refs produced phantom file_deps via populate_file_deps_from_call_edges in BOTH modes, so the filed batch-not-affected claim held only for the imports-derived path.

title: Method calls resolve by name only: x.unwrap() on external types misattributed to a same-named in-crate method
description:
Method-call resolution matches on the method NAME without receiver-type information, so a call like x.unwrap() where x is an external/std type (e.g. Option) resolves to an in-crate method of the same name when one exists (e.g. an inherent Thing::unwrap).

Effects:
1. Fabricated call edge: an unrelated caller appears to call the in-crate Thing::unwrap (over-attribution in callers/impact/reachability).
2. Hidden external call: the genuine external .unwrap() gets a symbol_id and its reference_name is nulled, so panic-points (WHERE reference_name IN ('unwrap','expect')) reports 0 — a false negative.

Repro:
  src/lib.rs:
    pub struct Thing; impl Thing { pub fn unwrap(&self) {} }
    pub fn use_external() { let x: Option<i32> = Some(1); x.unwrap(); }
    pub fn use_internal()  { let t = Thing; t.unwrap(); }
  After index: BOTH unwrap call refs have the SAME symbol_id (the in-crate Thing::unwrap); Panic Points Analysis

title: Glob and module re-exports: mark target public symbols as referenced
description:
## What to build

tethys-v1w8 (v1) records refs only for NAMED re-exports (pub use m::Name, groups, aliases). Two shapes are deferred here:

1. Glob re-exports (pub use m::*): semantically re-export every public symbol of the target module, so dead-code/visibility analyses should treat all of them as referenced. Requires enumerate-public-symbols-of-resolved-module machinery at resolve time (per-name ref synthesis or an equivalent suppression mechanism).
2. Module re-exports (pub use crate::db): the target is a module, not a symbol; its public symbols become reachable under the new path. Same suppression need as glob.

Evidence from the v1w8 probe (2026-07-01, .tethys-v1w8/): tethys's own source contains ZERO glob or module pub-use statements (80 re-exported names, all named/grouped), so deferral costs nothing today; generic workspaces will need this before dead-code Definite-tier claims are safe in the presence of glob re-exports.

Note: existing glob-import resolution machinery (cross-file callers via glob import) may be reusable.

## Blocked by

tethys-v1w8 (named re-export refs must exist first; this extends the same mechanism).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: pub use m::* makes every public symbol of m carry >=1 inbound ref (or a documented equivalent suppression consumed by dead-code)
- [ ] Fixture: pub use crate::db (module target) yields the same for db's public symbols
- [ ] Private symbols of the target module gain no refs
- [ ] Named re-export behavior (v1w8) unchanged (regression)
- [ ] Tests build their own index from fixture workspaces

title: C# global using propagation (C# 10 global using)
description: Carved out of tethys-usgf (the usgf loop scoped to `using static` only). `global using My.Models;` propagates that using to EVERY file in the compilation; tethys's per-file resolution model can't express it today (probe: a global using stores as a plain glob row in its own file only, no propagation). Mechanism: collect global usings workspace-wide and merge them into each C# file's effective using-set before resolution — architecturally distinct from the per-file ModuleContext model, so the highest blast radius of the three forms. Stays declining until implemented.

title: Add reference kind tracking to call_edges table
description:
The `call_edges` table currently only tracks caller→callee relationships with call counts, but loses information about the **type** of reference (call, import, type usage, etc.).

**Current limitation:**
- `get_callers` returns `reference_kinds: vec![ReferenceKind::Call]` as a hardcoded default
- The original join-based query used `GROUP_CONCAT(kind)` to aggregate actual reference kinds
- The `parse_reference_kinds` helper function was removed as dead code

**Why this matters:**
- Impact analysis may want to distinguish "function calls" from "type references"
- Security audits may care about data flow (calls) vs type dependencies (imports)
- Callers could filter by reference kind for more targeted queries

**Proposed enhancement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE call_edges ADD COLUMN kinds TEXT;
-- Store as comma-separated or JSON array: "call,call,import"
```

Or normalize with a separate table:
```sql
CREATE TABLE call_edge_kinds (
    caller_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    callee_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    kind TEXT NOT NULL,
    count INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    PRIMARY KEY (caller_symbol_id, callee_symbol_id, kind)
);
```

**Implementation notes:**
- Update `populate_call_edges` SQL to GROUP BY kind as well
- Restore `parse_reference_kinds` function or use JSON
- Update `get_callers`/`get_callees` to return actual kinds

**Trade-off:**
- More storage and slightly more complex queries
- But provides richer information for analysis

title: Visibility tightening: member- and module-level items (methods, struct fields, pub mod)
description: Follow-up to tethys-xoxq, which scopes to top-level item kinds (function, struct, enum, trait, type_alias, const). Member kinds need different semantics: trait-impl methods cannot be tightened (visibility inherited from trait), struct fields interact with constructors/patterns, and pub mod tightening changes reachability of everything inside. Discovered during xoxq design.

title: C# extractor doesn't index const / static-field / enum-member symbols (or their refs)
description: Probed in the usgf loop (.usgf/probe-findings.md 2026-06-07): the C# tree-sitter extractor emits only callable members as symbols and only call-shaped expressions as references. SCOPE NARROWED by tethys-xebx (PR #20, 2026-07-09): field_declaration symbols (const and static readonly included, kind struct_field, one symbol per declarator) and member-access reference expressions (field_access refs, per access level) now ship. REMAINING scope: (1) enum_member_declaration symbols — Color.Red still has no symbol row; (2) using-static disambiguation for non-method members — bare-identifier reads of using-static-imported consts/fields produce no refs (bare reads are a different grammar shape, see tethys-5uqz for the sibling implicit-this gap); (3) decide whether const fields deserve a distinct kind vs struct_field. Adjacent to tethys-itez / tethys-778r (C# parser completeness).

title: tethys: audit other UPSERT-only tables for stale-row growth (sibling of rivets-lcb6)
description:
Surfaced by code-reviewer during PR #65 multi-agent review. The rivets-lcb6 fix establishes the pattern: UPSERT-only tables (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE) need an explicit clear before full re-index, or 'ref_count'-style aggregates grow monotonically and removed-at-source rows persist as phantoms.

PR #65 fixed file_deps (and noted call_edges already had its clear_all_call_edges). Other tables in crates/tethys/src/db/ using ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE were NOT audited.

Likely candidates to grep (ON CONFLICT...DO UPDATE in crates/tethys/src/db/):
- imports (db/imports.rs?)
- refs (db/refs.rs)
- attributes (db/attributes.rs?)
- symbols' subordinate tables (enum variants, struct fields — added in PR #58)
- architecture / coupling tables (added in PR #60)
- any new table added after rivets-lcb6 lands

For each: classify as
  (a) UPSERT with monotonically-growing aggregate column → SAME BUG, needs clear_all_X + call from index_with_options
  (b) UPSERT but no growing aggregate → still has the 'stale row persists when source is deleted' subset of the bug
  (c) UPSERT but table is fully rebuilt from another source-of-truth each run → no bug

Deliverable: a comment in this issue listing every UPSERT-only table with its classification, then file follow-up issues for any (a) or (b) cases.

The 30.7% baseline-vs-resolved gap noted in project_resolver_baseline_2026_05_12.md may have contributions from this class of bug we haven't measured.

Source: PR #65 multi-agent review aggregate (code-reviewer 'Suggestion #3'/Strength).
acceptance_criteria:
* All UPSERT-only tables in crates/tethys/src/db/ enumerated with classification (a/b/c)
* Follow-up issues filed for each (a) or (b) classification
* If any (a) is found, regression-fence test added matching the file_deps_idempotency.rs pattern

title: Implement cycle detection for file dependencies
Populate parent_symbol_id for nested symbols
description:
The schema supports `parent_symbol_id` for hierarchical symbols (e.g., method inside struct) but it's always `None`. See `lib.rs:632` and `lib.rs:761`.

Populating this would enable queries like:
- "Show all methods of struct X"
- "Find all nested types in module Y"
- Symbol hierarchy navigation
notes: Closed: Shipped: PR #29 merged to main (ccb6cf3). parent_symbol_id populated at insert: parent_name plumbed through parse_file_static (both index paths), two-phase same-file container linkage in index_parsed_file_atomic (container kinds only per D-C; cross-file/collision -> NULL). Self-index 699 links, 0 cross-file; probe pair-set agreement fully decomposed (695 shared + 4 correct newtype-field extras). Fences: tests/parent_symbols.rs F-P1..F-P7 + extractor contract arms; deliberate 2-row golden update. Fixed in-branch: tethys-dl7l (absorbed). UNBLOCKS tethys-j2r1.

title: Deprecated-callers analysis: list call sites of #[deprecated] symbols (Rust)
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Act 1 roadmap, first stage (paired with visibility tightening).

## What to build

An analysis listing every reference site of symbols marked #[deprecated], so call sites can be migrated before the deprecated item is removed. End-to-end: deprecated-attribute detection during indexing (Rust symbol attribute extraction already exists), a facade query joining deprecated symbols to their inbound references, and a CLI subcommand with table and JSON output following the existing analysis-command conventions (panic-points, unused-imports are the prior art).

Findings here are factual — attribute present plus reference exists — so no confidence tiers are needed; include the deprecation note/since text when the attribute carries one.

C# ([Obsolete]) is explicitly a follow-up slice, not this one.

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: a #[deprecated] function called from another file lists that call site (caller symbol, file, line) via the CLI
- [ ] JSON output mode is stable and parseable
- [ ] Non-deprecated symbols never appear; a deprecated symbol with zero callers reports as clean (migration done)

title: MCP server for tethys tools
description:
Expose tethys functionality as MCP tools for AI agent integration. AI assistants like Claude/Cursor/Copilot can then query the indexed graph instead of grepping files, dramatically cutting tool-call count and context usage on long sessions.

Inspired by both Drift (50+ MCP tools across 7 layers) and KiroGraph (16 MCP tools, all auto-approved). KiroGraph's design observation drives most of the value: ''Kiro literally queries the graph instead of grepping files,'' so the index becomes context the model uses for every prompt.

**MVP tools (wrap existing Tethys API methods):**

| Tool | Description | Underlying API |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| tethys_status | Index stats, language breakdown | get_stats |
| tethys_symbol | Get symbol by qualified name | get_symbol |
| tethys_search | Search symbols by name/kind | search_symbols |
| tethys_callers | Who calls this symbol? | get_callers |
| tethys_callees | What does this symbol call? | get_symbol_dependencies |
| tethys_impact | Impact analysis for a symbol | get_symbol_impact |
| tethys_file_impact | Impact analysis for a file | get_impact |
| tethys_reachable | Forward/backward reachability | get_forward_reachable / get_backward_reachable |
| tethys_cycles | Detect dependency cycles | detect_cycles |
| tethys_panic_points | Find .unwrap()/.expect() calls | get_panic_points |
| tethys_affected_tests | Tests affected by changed files | get_affected_tests |

**v2 tools (depend on new analytics — see sibling issues):**

| Tool | Description | Depends on |
|------|-------------|------------|
| tethys_path | Shortest path between two symbols | new path-query feature |
| tethys_dead_code | Symbols with zero incoming refs | new dead-code feature |
| tethys_hotspots | Most-connected symbols | new hotspots feature |
| tethys_surprising | Non-obvious cross-file edges | new surprising-connections feature |
| tethys_type_hierarchy | Up/down extends-implements walk | new type-hierarchy feature |
| tethys_coupling | Per-package Ca/Ce/instability | new architecture-metrics feature |
| tethys_diff | Graph diff vs. saved snapshot | new snapshot/diff feature |

**Implementation plan:**
- New crates/tethys-mcp crate (binary + thin lib).
- Use rmcp (already in workspace via rivets-mcp).
- Each tool is a thin async wrapper around a Tethys method.
- Tools should default to auto-approved in the Claude MCP config so the model can call them freely.
- Coexists with rivets-mcp on a single MCP host.

**Benefits:**
- AI agents query codebase structure instead of grepping files.
- Integrates with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, and any MCP-compatible client.
- Complements rivets-mcp (issue tracker) and tethys's CLI.

## Adapter-seam constraint (added 2026-07-12, grill session — pi-lens lesson)

First acceptance criterion, before any tool ships: every MCP tool is exactly one `Tethys` facade method + one tool route — the MCP module never reaches into `crate::db`, `Index`, or resolution internals. The CLI-side fence (tethys-r4j2) extends to the `mcp` module in this issue's first slice, so the seam is guarded from day one rather than documented and eroded (pi-lens's index.ts god-adapter postmortem). A capability the facade lacks means a facade method is added first, then routed.

## 2026-07-12 pi-lens implementation review — reference implementations for the MCP surface

pi-lens runs the closest working analogue of this issue (a hand-rolled stdio MCP server over the same engine seam its primary host uses — mcp/server.ts, 16 tools). Mechanisms worth adopting, each with their source location:

**1. Per-field provenance in tool responses** (clients/module-report.ts:1707-1751): every response section is labeled with its source — symbols: syntax|none; imports: syntax|cached-review-graph|none; usedBy: cached-review-graph|none — plus a staleness field (fresh|unavailable) and the underlying artifact's built-at timestamp. Their MCP surface additionally converts a built-at older than 10 minutes into an explicit staleness note (mcp/server.ts:366-394) — while the always-warm host surface deliberately omits the note as noise. tethys translation: ADR-0003's store-the-strategy philosophy extended to reply envelopes — every MCP tool response carries index generated-at, per-section provenance (e.g. callers: resolved-refs; bands included/excluded), and the CONTEXT.md query-standing field (confirmed/indeterminate) from tethys-09wx.

**2. Never block a query on a build** (clients/word-index.ts:540-592, lens-engine.ts:191-221): a cold query returns available:false plus a retry hint immediately, and kicks a background build deduped per workspace (an in-flight set keyed on canonical root, cleaned in finally). A warm in-memory artifact is preferred over the persisted one because disk lags the debounced persist. tethys translation: an MCP query against a missing/stale index returns indeterminate-with-reason plus triggers (or suggests) reindex — never a silent empty, never a blocking multi-second index inside a tool call.

**3. Background work lifecycle — fire, park, settle, re-park** (clients/runtime-coordinator.ts:414-465): long analyses are kicked off unawaited, their promises parked; at a natural checkpoint they are drained with a bounded race (their budget 5s); stragglers are RE-PARKED for the next checkpoint, never dropped (they measured graph builds up to ~19s that must surface eventually). Their "already reported" state is a drain-and-clear queue (consume = clear), which is what keeps repeat notifications from ballooning. tethys translation: the auto-reindex loop for a long-lived server.

**4. Workspace-root resolution guard checklist** (clients/knip-client.ts:97-103, their #296/#250 burns): marker walk-up; STOP at VCS boundary (.git/.hg/.svn); home-directory ceiling; and null-not-cwd fallback (a bare cwd fallback once made a tool recurse the whole home tree). Audit our workspace discovery (src/cargo.rs) against all four failure modes when the MCP server starts accepting arbitrary roots.

**5. If the server keeps LSP clients warm** (clients/lsp/index.ts:105-108, 683-777, 861-873): dedup clients by server-id + workspace-root (their RustWorkspaceRoot walks to the [workspace] Cargo.toml so all members share ONE rust-analyzer — the wrong root means one server per crate); initialize-timeout kills the process tree (no zombies); spawn failures get exponential-backoff cooldown with permanent disable after 5 consecutive failures; an orphan reaper guards recycled PIDs before killing.

**Seam reminder (2026-07-12 grill session, unchanged):** every tool = one Tethys facade method + one route; the fence from tethys-r4j2 extends to the mcp module in this issue's first slice.

title: Re-export references: pub use targets carry inbound refs
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Act 1 resolver-correctness prerequisite. Identified in the June 2026 indexing-strategy review as the last unfiled gap in that family.

## What to build

A re-export site (pub use path::Symbol) currently produces no reference to the target symbol, so a symbol consumed only through its re-export has zero inbound refs and is a false positive for every reference-consuming analysis: dead code flags it, visibility tightening wants to privatize it, hotspots undercount it.

Record a reference from the re-export site to the target symbol during extraction and resolve it in Pass 2, end-to-end: after indexing a fixture, the target is reachable by name through the standard query surfaces (callers, the refs_named view).

Per the PRD error posture (suppressions, not accusations): be conservative. Glob re-exports (pub use x::star) need an explicit recorded decision — either a ref per resolvable name or a documented out-of-scope note with rationale — not a guess.

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately.
design: DESIGN (falsifiable-design 2026-07-01, awaiting approval): extractor emits kind='reexport' refs per non-glob leaf name at pub-use sites; Pass 2 resolves via existing explicit-import machinery. NOT Pass-2-from-imports (probe: imports table persists neither is_reexport nor line). Consumers safe by construction: in_symbol_id NULL excludes call_edges + panic-points. Probe/oracle agree 80/80 on self-index; 9 real zero-ref re-exported symbols found; latent file_deps gap (re-export-only imports) fixed as C10. Glob/module re-exports deferred to tethys-pv7w. Full design + 14-claim falsification table: .tethys-v1w8/design.md
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: a symbol referenced ONLY via pub use re-export has >= 1 inbound ref after indexing, visible via callers and the refs_named view
- [ ] A symbol both re-exported and called directly is not double-counted (regression fence on existing ref counts)
- [ ] Explicit recorded decision for glob re-exports: implemented, or documented out-of-scope with rationale
- [ ] Existing bare-call, method-call, and qualified-call extraction unchanged (regression fences pass)
- [ ] Tests build their own index from fixture workspaces, never an ambient DB
notes: Closed: Implemented on feat/tethys-v1w8-reexport-refs (7 commits, 78dfb7b..HEAD). Extractor emits kind='reexport' refs per named non-glob leaf at pub-use sites; resolution reuses explicit-import machinery unchanged. All 14 design claims verified (amendments recorded in .tethys-v1w8/design.md): probe Q2 empty — the 9 zero-ref re-exported symbols on the self-index all carry refs now. Fences: tests/reexport_refs.rs (7), emission stress test, kind round-trip, both golden arms. Residuals filed: tethys-rylk (group-alias drop), tethys-z9mr (bare-segment path decline — covers the remaining lib.rs->unused_imports.rs dep gap), tethys-sp24 (aliased corroboration); glob/module semantics deferred to tethys-pv7w (blocks-linked). Unblocks tethys-xoxq (visibility tightening).

title: tethys: non-transactional clear in index_with_options leaves DB degraded on mid-pipeline panic
description:
Both clear_all_call_edges (indexing.rs:424) and clear_all_file_deps (indexing.rs:138, added in rivets-lcb6) run outside any explicit transaction. If the indexing pipeline panics or is interrupted between the clear and the subsequent re-population:

- file_deps is empty (or partially populated)
- call_edges is empty (if the panic happens after line 424)

Until the next complete index run, downstream queries (coupling, callers, cycles, impact analysis) operate on degraded data.

This is pre-existing for call_edges; rivets-lcb6 makes file_deps adopt the same pattern (the alternative — leaving file_deps stale — has worse properties). So this issue documents a known risk shared by both, not a regression introduced by lcb6.

Surfaced by claude-review on PR #65.

Likely fix shape: wrap the clear + populate work in a transaction so partial failure rolls back. Connection-level: BEGIN / COMMIT around the relevant indexing.rs section. SQLite WAL mode supports this; the question is what 'partial failure' means in the current architecture (batch_writer commits in chunks, so wrapping the whole pipeline in one transaction may not be feasible without changes there).

Out of scope here; file before fix work begins so the design considers it.

Reference acceptance criteria for the eventual fix:
- Test: index a workspace, abort mid-pipeline (panic injection), assert file_deps + call_edges are EITHER both populated from prior state OR both empty — no half-populated states.
- Test: under normal operation, no perf regression compared to current non-transactional pattern.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] file_deps and call_edges remain consistent (both old-state or both new-state) across pipeline abort
- [ ] No significant indexing perf regression
- [ ] Both clear_all_file_deps and clear_all_call_edges share the same transactional discipline

title: PRD: tethys scope — code-health analyzer first (dead-code roadmap), agent-facing backend second (MCP + view surface)
description:
# PRD: tethys scope — code-health analyzer first, agent-facing backend second

## Problem Statement

As tethys's maintainer, I have two intertwined problems.

First, I want to trust tethys enough to act on its findings — to delete code it says is dead, tighten visibility it says is over-exposed, and know which changes ship untested — without fear that a false positive breaks something a naive reference count didn't see. Today the analyses I most want (dead code, untested code) are blocked behind known resolver-correctness gaps: functions used only as callbacks look dead, method calls resolve by bare name and fabricate edges, re-exported API carries no inbound references.

Second, my backlog stopped telling me what matters. Issues accumulated from three inspiration waves (drift-inspired, kirograph-inspired, PR-review fallout) plus repeated TODO-scans, with no product definition to triage against. Roughly nine open features (MCP server, hotspots, snapshot/diff, HTML export, path queries, type hierarchy, auto-sync, surprising connections, Python/TS/Go expansion) cannot be prioritized because nothing on record says what tethys is *for*. Meanwhile, external tools (kiro-control-center) already consume the index by querying SQLite directly — and have been burned doing so (the make_service incident, where querying a raw column instead of a semantic surface produced vacuous results and a phantom bug report).

## Solution

Declare tethys a two-act product and triage every open feature against it.

**Act 1 — code-health analyzer.** tethys is a code intelligence engine whose flagship deliverable is a suite of trustworthy health analyses for Rust and C#: unused-imports (shipped) → visibility tightening + deprecated-callers → untested-code → dead code. "Trustworthy" is a hard requirement, defined by the established error posture: findings are suppressions, not accusations — a Definite-tier finding must be near-zero false-positive, and anything uncertain is downgraded to a Maybe tier rather than asserted. The resolver-correctness bugs that would poison these analyses are in scope as gating prerequisites, not optional polish.

**Act 2 — agent-facing query backend.** Once dead code ships with a clean self-index oracle, tethys exposes its analyses and queries to coding agents: an MCP server wrapping the same library facade the CLI uses, and a documented SQL-view query surface (the refs_named pattern) so external consumers never touch raw tables again. Act 2 is deliberately sequenced second: publishing an agent surface over an index that fabricates edges would export the false positives to every consumer.

Everything else is triaged Later or Out of Scope below.

## User Stories

1. As a solo maintainer, I want a dead-code report whose Definite tier has near-zero false positives, so that I can delete flagged symbols without auditing each one by hand.
2. As a solo maintainer, I want findings tiered by confidence (Definite vs Maybe), so that uncertain cases read as suggestions to investigate rather than accusations to act on.
3. As a solo maintainer, I want functions referenced only as values (callbacks, stored fn pointers) to carry inbound references, so that callback-only functions never appear dead.
4. As a solo maintainer, I want method calls attributed to the right receiver's method rather than any same-named symbol, so that calling unwrap on an Option doesn't register as a call to my in-crate unwrap.
5. As a solo maintainer, I want re-exported symbols (pub use targets) to carry inbound references, so that my crate's public API surface never appears dead.
6. As a solo maintainer, I want trait-implementation and interface-implementation methods linked to their definitions, so that an impl method with zero direct call sites is suppressed from dead-code findings rather than flagged.
7. As a solo maintainer, I want a visibility-tightening report (public items that could be crate-private), so that the public surface shrinks to what external code actually uses.
8. As a solo maintainer, I want a deprecated-callers report, so that I can migrate every call site before removing a deprecated item.
9. As a solo maintainer, I want an untested-code report computed by backward traversal from test roots, so that I know which code no test can reach before I refactor it.
10. As a solo maintainer, I want analyses to produce the same quality of results for C# as for Rust, so that both indexed languages get equal health signals.
11. As a CI pipeline, I want affected-tests for a set of changed files, so that PR builds run only the tests that could break.
12. As a CI pipeline, I want stable JSON output from every analysis command, so that gates parse structured results instead of scraping tables.
13. As a CI pipeline, I want a fast staleness check and incremental re-index, so that per-PR indexing doesn't pay full-workspace cost.
14. As a solo maintainer, I want files deleted from disk purged from the index on the next run, so that orphan rows never contribute phantom edges to any analysis.
15. As a coding agent, I want name-based reference queries answered through a documented view, so that I get correct results without reverse-engineering resolution semantics from raw columns.
16. As a coding agent, I want MCP tools mirroring the analysis commands, so that I can query the index in-conversation without shelling out to the CLI.
17. As an external tool author, I want the SQL views declared as the stable query contract, so that internal schema changes don't silently break my integration.
18. As an external tool author, I want not-found errors discriminated by kind (package vs file vs symbol), so that my tooling can react appropriately instead of parsing message strings.
19. As a reviewer, I want callers and impact analyses with consistent depth control on both commands, so that blast-radius answers are scoped the same way everywhere.
20. As a solo maintainer, I want indexing failures surfaced in the final stats rather than only in logs, so that a partially-written index is impossible to mistake for a complete one.
21. As a solo maintainer, I want a coupling/instability view per package, so that refactor targets are chosen by blast radius rather than intuition (shipped; audit the remainder).

## Implementation Decisions

- **Two-act sequencing is a gate, not a preference.** Act 2 (MCP server, view-surface hardening) does not begin until the dead-code analysis ships with a clean self-index oracle. The single exception: discriminated not-found error variants land immediately before the MCP work as its enabling change, as already recorded on that issue.
- **Analyses are CLI subcommands over the SQLite index, backed by facade methods.** Every analysis is a method on the Tethys facade, exposed as a CLI subcommand with a JSON output mode. Act 2 MCP tools wrap the same facade methods — never raw SQL — so CLI and MCP cannot drift.
- **The external query surface is SQL views, not tables.** The refs_named view established the pattern: additive views with semantic columns are the contract for external consumers; raw tables are internal and may change. New query capabilities for consumers land as views.
- **Error posture: suppressions, not accusations.** Conservative guards (textual word-boundary scans, confidence downgrades) are preferred over completeness. A finding tier is part of the public contract of each analysis.
- **Dead-code traversal reads the refs table, not just call_edges**, because call-edge population skips references that occur outside any enclosing symbol.
- **Resolver-correctness prerequisites gate dead code**: fn-as-value references, name-only method resolution, phantom import-usage marking, self-crate qualified-path resolution, re-export target references (to be filed — identified in the June review but never ticketed), and orphan-file cleanup in streaming mode.
- **Type hierarchy (extends/implements edges) is promoted from speculative feature to Act 1 suppression infrastructure**: it exists to keep trait/interface impl methods out of dead-code findings, and is scoped to that need.
- **Incremental indexing (staleness check + changed-file-only re-index) is Act 1 infrastructure** for CI usability. Known design constraint to honor: naive single-file re-index breaks inbound resolved references via the symbol-delete cascade, so the design must reconcile or re-resolve affected refs.
- **Roadmap order within Act 1**: visibility tightening + deprecated-callers → untested-code → dead code. Untested-code intentionally precedes dead code because it exercises the same reverse-traversal machinery with a cheaper failure mode (a wrong "untested" claim wastes a test run; a wrong "dead" claim deletes live code).
- **Kirograph-wave triage**: hotspots, path queries, snapshot/diff, and auto-sync are Later (post-Act-2, unscheduled; auto-sync additionally blocked on incremental indexing). The architecture/coupling feature is substantially shipped — audit its remaining acceptance criteria and close or narrow it.

## Testing Decisions

- **A good test exercises external behavior at the highest seam**: run the CLI subcommand with JSON output against a fixture workspace and assert on the parsed result. Internal tables are asserted only when a view is itself the published contract.
- **Every test builds its own index from a fixture workspace — never queries an ambient DB.** This is the recorded lesson from the refs_named work, where a stale on-disk index nearly invalidated probe results.
- **Self-index oracle as a CI fence**: tethys compiles warning-free, so any Definite-tier finding by unused-imports (and later dead-code) against tethys itself is by definition a false positive. This fence runs in CI for every analysis that has a compiler-adjacent ground truth.
- **Golden-content fences protect performance refactors**: byte-identical index-content dumps (the idxperf pattern) pin that optimization changes are behavior-preserving.
- **Risky changes go through the falsifiable-design loop**: a probe that produces the proposed output against the real codebase, an independent oracle computing the same answer a different way, and agreement before implementation — the workflow already used for the indexing-performance and refs_named work.
- **Prior art**: the idxperf golden tests, the refs_named regression fences, the pass-2 qualified-path tests, and the shared fixture-workspace helper (whose three copies are already ticketed for consolidation).

## Out of Scope

- **Interactive HTML graph export** — a presentation-layer product with its own maintenance surface; no analysis depends on it.
- **Surprising-connections scoring** (cross-file edges ranked by path distance) — a speculative heuristic with no consumer; revisit only if a concrete workflow demands it.
- **Python/TypeScript/Go language expansion** — every new language multiplies the resolver-correctness surface that currently gates the flagship analyses. Explicit re-entry condition: dead code shipped with a clean self-index oracle on both Rust and C#.
- **Code modification** — tethys remains read-only analysis; it reports, it never edits.
- **Re-speccing the indexing/resolution engine** — the engine is governed by its filed correctness bugs and regression fences; this PRD adjudicates scope, not engine design.
- **Expanded LSP integration** — the existing opt-in LSP enrichment stays as-is this cycle.

## Further Notes

- This PRD follows the 2026-07-01 backlog cleanup: 16 issues closed (5 duplicate clusters, 2 already-shipped, 2 consolidation bundles created). Process rule going forward: search the tracker before filing — every duplicate cluster came from scan-and-file sessions that skipped the search.
- Many older issues cite pre-restructure paths (crates/tethys/...); the workspace is now flat. Treat file:line references in issues created before June 2026 as hints, not addresses.
- One prerequisite identified in the June indexing review remains unfiled: re-export (pub use) targets carry no inbound references. File it as part of Act 1 prerequisite work.
- The old umbrella epic ("Codebase Intelligence Engine") predates this scope decision; issues parented to it should be re-triaged against this PRD's acts when picked up.

title: Aliased re-export-only import produces no file_dep: corroboration looks up the alias, reexport refs carry the original name
description:
compute_dependencies' import-usage check tests refs_set.contains(alias.unwrap_or(name)) — for an aliased import it looks up ONLY the alias. Re-export refs (tethys-v1w8) deliberately carry the ORIGINAL name (design C3: resolution needs the name that matches the import row and symbols table). Net effect: a file whose only use of an import is an aliased re-export (pub use a::B as C;) fails corroboration and gets no file_deps edge, while the unaliased form corroborates fine.

Narrow residual: requires aliased + re-export-only + no other corroborating usage in the file. Candidate fix: is_used tests name OR alias membership in refs_set (marginal phantom risk: a body ref matching the ORIGINAL name of an aliased import is not valid Rust scope, so a hit implies another binding — evaluate against the msn0 conservatism posture). Alternative: corroborate is_reexport imports as used-by-definition — requires is_reexport at dep-computation time (available fresh-parse; hardcoded false in the stored/streaming variant since the imports table does not persist it).

Discovered during tethys-v1w8 slice 6 while reading compute_dependencies to explain an unexpectedly-green fence.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: pub use a::B as C; with no other usage yields a file_dep to B's file
- [ ] Plain unused aliased import still yields no dep (msn0 no-spill regression)
- [ ] Batch and streaming variants agree
notes: Closed: Fixed: PR #17. Repro fences: aliased_reexport_only_import_corroborates_dep (batch+streaming) + bare_aliased_import_with_unrelated_original_name_stays_edgeless (pins the reexport-kind scoping). Streaming parity gap filed separately.

title: tethys: test format_uri percent-encodes literal % in paths
description:
PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET includes .add(b'%'), so a literal % in a path encodes as %25. No test exercises this. A regression removing .add(b'%') would produce a URI that parses but resolves to the wrong file (or double-decodes downstream).

Rare in Rust source paths but load-bearing for the RFC 3986 round-trip claim.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (pr-test-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Test added to crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs#tests
- [ ] Input path contains a literal % character
- [ ] Asserts the % is encoded as %25 in the resulting URI
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-w3z7 (transport.rs format_uri hardening bundle); full details preserved here.

title: Consolidate three copies of workspace_with_files test helper
description: Three independent copies of the workspace_with_files test fixture helper exist in the tethys integration test suite. PR #63 (rivets-6aoc) had to update all three with the same Cargo.toml auto-write logic; future test-infrastructure changes will keep paying this triplicate cost.
design:
## Current state

Three near-identical `workspace_with_files` functions:

1. `crates/tethys/tests/common/mod.rs` (shared, intended canonical location)
2. `crates/tethys/tests/indexing.rs` (local copy, 77 callers in this one file)
3. `crates/tethys/tests/test_topology.rs` (local copy)

All three now (post-PR #63) have similar auto-write-default-Cargo.toml logic with slight string differences:
- common writes "test_workspace"
- indexing writes "test_workspace"
- test_topology writes "test_topology"

## Folds in S2 from PR #63 review

The Cargo.toml exact-string match in the helpers (`files.iter().any(|(p, _)| *p == "Cargo.toml")`) is fragile: a caller passing "./Cargo.toml" would trigger a silent double-write. Normalize via `Path::file_name()` once during consolidation.

## Proposed work

1. Make `tests/common/mod.rs::workspace_with_files` the canonical helper.
2. Delete the local copies in `tests/indexing.rs` and `tests/test_topology.rs`.
3. Update all callers in those files to `use common::workspace_with_files;` (add `mod common;`).
4. Normalize Cargo.toml detection in the canonical helper via `Path::file_name()` rather than exact-string comparison.
5. Verify all integration tests still pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Single workspace_with_files in tests/common/mod.rs
- [ ] indexing.rs and test_topology.rs use the common helper via mod common
- [ ] Cargo.toml detection uses Path::file_name(), not exact-string match
- [ ] cargo nextest run -p tethys passes
- [ ] cargo clippy + cargo fmt clean

title: affected-tests: distinguish confirmed-empty from indeterminate (query standing)
description:
Adopt the affirmative-clean posture (pi-lens lesson; grill session 2026-07-12) for the flagship CI command: an empty result the index cannot stand behind must never look like confirmed-clean.

Contract (decided):
- Confirmed result: exit 0, stdout as today (empty stdout = confirmed no affected tests).
- Indeterminate result: exit 2 (distinct from error exit 1), machine-readable reason(s) on stderr; stdout still carries whatever tests WERE found. Fires regardless of result count — a stale index with 5 hits is still under-complete.
- v1 indeterminate triggers, both deterministic and zero-false-alarm: (a) a changed file has no row in the index; (b) a changed file's on-disk mtime is newer than its indexed state (reindex.rs staleness machinery already computes this).
- Explicitly OUT of v1: resolution-quality triggers (unresolved/speculative ratios) — gated behind Act 1 resolver work per PRD tethys-l6nt; a fuzzy trigger would train CI recipes to ignore exit 2.
- README documents the CI recipe: exit 0 + empty = skip (confirmed); exit 2 = run full suite (fail-open with signal). Mechanism in the tool, policy in the consumer.

Vocabulary: CONTEXT.md "Query standing" (confirmed / indeterminate) — added in the same session; keep terms aligned.

Related: tethys-zwaz (output envelope convergence — the exit contract should compose with, not duplicate, the envelope work). PRD user stories 11-12.

Current behavior evidence: src/cli/affected_tests.rs:31-36 — empty result with --names-only prints nothing, exits 0, indistinguishable from confirmed-clean.

title: Type hierarchy: extract and traverse extends/implements edges
description:
Add type-hierarchy queries — walk inheritance and trait/interface implementation chains upward (base types) or downward (derived types).

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_type_hierarchy tool.

**Languages:**
- Rust: impl X for Y, trait X: Y + Z, struct X<T: Bound>
- C#: class X : BaseClass, IInterface, IGeneric<T>

**Schema additions:**
- New ReferenceKind variants: Extends, Implements (already partly modeled in ReferenceKind enum — check current state).
- Tree-sitter extractors for both languages emit these edges during indexing.
- Reuse the existing refs table; no new tables needed.

**Why this depends on rivets-aay4:**
For methods, we want to walk ''method M of class X'' to its overridden version in BaseClass. That requires knowing which class a method belongs to, which is parent_symbol_id. Without it, we can do class-to-class hierarchy but not method-to-method override resolution.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::get_type_hierarchy(symbol: &str, direction: HierarchyDirection) -> Result<TypeHierarchy>
- enum HierarchyDirection { Up, Down, Both }

**CLI:**
- tethys hierarchy <SYMBOL> [--direction up|down|both]
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Rust extractor emits Extends/Implements edges for impl blocks and trait bounds
- [ ] C# extractor emits Extends/Implements edges for class inheritance and interface lists
- [ ] get_type_hierarchy() API method
- [ ] CLI subcommand tethys hierarchy
- [ ] Tests on a Rust trait fixture and a C# class-hierarchy fixture
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_type_hierarchy (sibling rivets-o4re)

title: Converge analysis-command CLI output: envelope fences, BrokenPipe-safe writes, shared display helpers
description:
Filed from /code-review of PR #9 (tethys-jdly): panic-points, unused-imports, and deprecated-callers each hand-roll the same output shape — a local JsonOutput {summary, <payload>} + JsonSummary + to_string_pretty + Error::Internal block, and the '='.repeat(63) header / '  {:<28}{}' summary-line ritual in human mode. Rule-of-three duplication (Fowler), deliberately not fixed in-PR because the three commands are not behaviorally identical: deprecated-callers (newest) renders to String and does one BrokenPipe-guarded write via super::ignore_broken_pipe, while the older two println! per line — so 'tethys panic-points | head' can still die mid-report.

Converge toward the NEWEST pattern, in this order (extraction last, so the shared helper encodes the converged behavior instead of freezing the oldest):

1. Add binary-level envelope fences for panic-points and unused-imports (CARGO_BIN_EXE_tethys; deprecated-callers already has cli_json_envelope_stable_and_parseable from PR #9). These make step 2/3 refactors provable.
2. Migrate panic-points and unused-imports to render-to-String + single guarded write (the deprecated_callers.rs shape).
3. Extract src/cli/display.rs: report header, summary-line formatter, to_pretty_json (serialize + Error::Internal mapping), and the guarded emit; point all three commands at it.

Do NOT genericize the JsonSummary structs or unify payload keys — the differing summary fields and per-command payload key (panic_points / unused_imports / deprecated) are domain content and self-describing JSON, not duplication.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] panic-points and unused-imports each have a binary-level test pinning their {summary, <payload>} JSON envelope and byte-stability across invocations
- [ ] tethys panic-points | head and tethys unused-imports | head exit 0 on early pipe close (single guarded write)
- [ ] src/cli/display.rs absorbs header, summary-line, JSON-serialize plumbing, and guarded emit; all three analysis commands use it
- [ ] JSON envelopes are byte-identical before/after the refactor (the step-1 fences prove it)

title: Inline module bodies not indexed: MOD_ITEM arm skips declaration_list, dropping unit tests (is_test) and their refs
description:
## Symptom
The self-index flags only ~330 `is_test` symbols (all in `tests/`), versus
~842 test functions in source (`#[test]` src/ 467 + tests/ 353, plus
`#[tokio::test]`/`#[rstest]`; `cargo nextest` reports 843 cases). Every unit
test living in a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { … }` block is absent from the index.

## Root cause
`src/languages/rust.rs:954` — the `MOD_ITEM` arm of `extract_symbols_recursive`
records the module as a `SymbolKind::Module` symbol via
`extract_simple_definition` but does NOT recurse into the module body
(`declaration_list`). Compare `IMPL_ITEM` (src/languages/rust.rs:917) and the
`_` default arm (src/languages/rust.rs:959), which both recurse. Result: every
symbol declared inside an inline `mod { … }` block is dropped.

## Evidence (self-index, freshly rebuilt from current source)
- 26 `tests` module SHELL symbols exist (kind=module); their bodies are empty.
- 0 unit-test functions indexed: `is_excluded_dir_allows_lib`,
  `normalize_path_is_idempotent`, `extracts_simple_function` are not symbols.
- 212 `src/` function symbols — all product top-level functions.
- 4449 refs originate from `is_test` symbols — all from `tests/` integration tests.

## Impact
- Blocks tethys-y3bx (untested-code): unit tests are absent as reachability
  roots AND their reference edges into product code are absent from `refs`, so
  code covered only by unit tests would be falsely reported "untested."
- Any product code declared inside an inline module is also dropped.
- Contradicts tethys-o6x7's stated intent ("index all symbols including test
  functions").

## Blast radius (why this needs a design pass, not a one-line patch)
Recursing into inline module bodies makes EVERY analysis start seeing test
code (unused-imports, visibility-tightening, deprecated-callers, dead-code,
coupling, counts). The `is_test` flag is the existing lever for include/exclude,
but each analysis needs an explicit decision, and symbol/ref counts (and golden
fences) will shift. Ship through the gilfoyle loop.

## Repro
```
tethys index -w .
sqlite3 .rivets/index/tethys.db "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM symbols WHERE is_test=1;"   # 330
grep -rn -E '^\s*#\[test\]' src tests | wc -l                                      # 820
```

## Fix sketch
Recurse `MOD_ITEM` into its `declaration_list` (mirror `IMPL_ITEM`). Oracle for
the fix: post-fix `is_test` count matches the independent source test-fn count;
known unit tests become symbols with `is_test=1`. Add a fence asserting a
`#[cfg(test)] mod tests` unit test is indexed. Decide per-analysis whether test
symbols are included or excluded (probably exclude from product-health analyses
via `is_test`, include as roots for reachability/untested/affected-tests).
notes: Closed: Shipped: PR #18 merged to main (a06e5b0). MOD_ITEM now recurses into inline module bodies; self-index is_test 330->813 (src/ 0->464), unit-test edges attach. Fences: test_topology::inline_module_indexing + visibility::same_crate_unit_test_usage_does_not_suppress_tightening_candidate. Filed: tethys-0nar (proptest fns), tethys-m7zm (analyses test policy). Unblocks tethys-y3bx.

title: Resolution provenance: persist strategy + confidence band on every resolved ref
description:
Motivation (comparative review vs codebase-memory-mcp, 2026-07-01): CBM persists {strategy, confidence, candidates} on every CALLS edge, banded high/medium/speculative (their registry.c strategy chain: import_map 0.95 ... fuzzy 0.40). One graph then serves both consumers: precision-demanding queries filter speculative edges OUT; recall-demanding analyses keep them IN. For tethys dead code this is exactly the endorsed posture — "errors must be suppressions, not accusations": a speculative "maybe someone calls this" edge should SUPPRESS a dead-code finding while staying excludable from callers/impact/panic-points.

tethys already has the strategy ladder — Pass 1 same-file maps (src/db/files.rs), Pass 2 try_resolve_reference arms in order: explicit import -> glob import -> same-crate prefix -> unique-workspace -> qualified_module_fallback (src/resolve.rs), Pass 3 LSP — but discards which arm fired. A ref bound by unscoped unique-name fallback is indistinguishable from one bound via an explicit import. That erasure is what makes the phantom-edge class (tethys-53iv, tethys-msn0, tethys-3i35) invisible: the fabricated bindings are precisely the ones that would land in the speculative band.

NOTE: this epic does NOT fix tethys-53iv. It labels the class. It changes 53iv's cheapest acceptable fix from "decline method-call binding entirely" to "bind but band as speculative" (friendlier to dead-code recall). 53iv/msn0/3i35 remain their own fixes.

Slices (each its own PR; slice 2 through the gilfoyle loop — it touches the machinery the 6rlu subtractive sweep proved is booby-trapped):

1. Design/ADR + CONTEXT.md glossary. Define the strategy enum (same_file, explicit_import, glob_import, same_crate_prefix, unique_workspace, qualified_module_fallback, lsp) and the strategy->band mapping. Key decision to settle in the ADR: store strategy TEXT only and DERIVE the band in views (recommended — revisable without re-index; CBM's fixed numeric confidences are essentially arbitrary, the strategy label is the real information) vs store a numeric confidence column. Glossary entries: "resolution strategy", "confidence band", "speculative edge".

2. Write path. Add column(s) on refs (decide: does call_edges carry its own copy or join through refs? populate_call_edges derives from refs). Thread strategy through Pass 1 same-file resolution, every try_resolve_reference arm, and Pass 3 LSP single-row resolve. The resolve UPDATE SQL was unified into one seam in 4b5e7c4 — widen that seam, do not fork it. Known churn: idxperf_golden.rs byte-identical fences and canonical-dump oracle change with DB content — regold deliberately, do not discover it.

3. Query surface. View sibling to refs_named exposing the band; panic-points and callers gain the ability to exclude speculative bindings.

4. Consumer (deferrable). Dead-code traversal treats speculative edges as suppressions. Cannot land before dead code exists — this is an acceptance criterion on the dead-code work, tracked here only so it is not forgotten.

Origin: architecture comparison with ../codebase-memory-mcp (their pass_calls.c persists per-edge provenance; see also their >256-candidates ambiguity cap and candidate-count confidence penalty as graded alternatives to our binary decline-on->=2).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] ADR merged: strategy enum + strategy->band mapping + store-strategy-derive-band decision
- [ ] CONTEXT.md glossary entries: resolution strategy, confidence band, speculative edge
- [ ] Every resolution write path (Pass 1 same-file, each Pass 2 arm, Pass 3 LSP) records its strategy through the unified UPDATE seam
- [ ] View exposes band; panic-points and callers can exclude speculative bindings
- [ ] idxperf_golden fences regolded deliberately; new fence pins strategy values on a fixture crate
- [ ] Dead-code work references speculative-as-suppression as an acceptance criterion
notes: Closed: Slices 1-3 shipped: ADR-0003 (PR #12), write path (PR #13, closed tethys-xvlw), query surface (PR #14: refs_banded view + callers --exclude-speculative). AC AMENDMENT (approved at slice-3 design pause): panic-points exclusion is probe-proven vacuous — it matches unresolved-name refs (strategy NULL by construction; 1761 rows, zero resolved unwrap/expect refs on the self-index); evidence in .tethys-9z7i/findings.md slice-3 addendum; the change to what panic-points sees belongs to tethys-53iv's bind-but-band fix. Slice 4 (speculative-as-suppression) transferred to tethys-dvsw's ACs per this epic's own instruction. Self-index provenance: speculative band = 31% of resolved refs; 34% of call edges were speculative-only.

title: tethys: expose --depth flag on `callers --transitive` for parity with impact
description:
PR #57 wired `--depth` through `tethys impact`, but `tethys callers --transitive` still calls `get_symbol_impact(symbol, None)` unconditionally. Users will reasonably expect the same flag on both commands.

Add a `--depth` CLI flag on `callers` and pass it through to `get_symbol_impact`.

title: Add Serialize/Deserialize derives to architecture types
description:
The architecture types added in rivets-byie (Package, PackageId, PackageSource, CouplingMetrics, CouplingSort, PackageDependency, CouplingDetail, ArchStats) don''t derive Serialize/Deserialize, while every other public type in tethys::types does (SymbolId, FileId, Language, Symbol, Reference, etc.).

The CLI's JSON output is currently hand-rolled via serde_json::json!{} macros, so this works fine for the CLI. But:
- Library consumers (rivets-mcp, future MCP tools) can't round-trip these types through serde.
- It's inconsistent with the crate-wide convention.

**Fix:** Add #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] to all eight architecture types in crates/tethys/src/types.rs. PackageId should also use #[serde(transparent)] like SymbolId and FileId. Adjust the CLI json! literals to delegate to the serde derives where simpler.

Found during final review of rivets-byie.

title: Integrate LSP into index --lsp
description:
When `--lsp` flag is set during indexing:

1. After Pass 2, identify still-unresolved references
2. For each, call LSP goto_definition to find the target
3. Update reference with resolved symbol_id

LSP is spawned lazily, kept alive for batch queries, shutdown on completion.

title: Reachability analysis (forward/backward data flow)
notes: Primitives already exist - just needs BFS wrapper. Removed dependency on call_edges table since we can use existing get_callers/get_callees.

title: Interactive HTML graph export
description:
Generate a static HTML/CSS/JS bundle that visualizes the indexed graph for human exploration. Three files served from a local directory, no server required, works offline.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph export build / start commands.

**Output:**
- .rivets/index/export/index.html — single HTML file
- .rivets/index/export/app.css
- .rivets/index/export/app.js — bundled with the graph data inlined as a const

**Visualization features (in priority order):**
1. Force-directed layout with color-coded nodes by kind, edge labels by kind.
2. Click-to-focus: click a node to inspect its details (kind, file:line, signature, degree).
3. Search box: filter nodes by name / qualified name / file path.
4. Path mode: click two nodes to find and highlight the shortest path between them.
5. Legend with click-to-hide for node kinds and edge kinds.
6. Degree-threshold filter slider.
7. Cluster mode: group nodes by directory.

**Lower priority (v2):**
- PNG export of current view.
- Heat-map mode coloring nodes by file mtime.
- Analytics charts (top-N most connected, kind distribution, degree histogram).

**Library choice:**
- Cytoscape.js (mature, MIT, ~300KB minified) is the best fit — covers force-directed, custom rendering, click handlers, path-finding, and clustering out of the box.
- Alternative: D3-force directly (more code but no large dep).

**Why P4 (lower priority):**
This is mostly orthogonal to indexing and the AI-assistant story. It's polish for human exploration. Defer until the analytical features (hotspots, surprising, coupling, etc.) are in place — the export then becomes a natural human counterpart to those CLI tools.

**CLI:**
- tethys export build [--out DIR] [--include-contains]
- tethys export start [--out DIR]    (build + open in browser)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] tethys export build produces a self-contained directory of html/css/js
- [ ] Force-directed layout with color-coded nodes
- [ ] Click-to-focus and node detail panel
- [ ] Search by name / qualified name / file
- [ ] Shortest-path mode (depends on path-query feature, but can fall back to BFS in JS)
- [ ] Works offline (no CDN; deps bundled)
- [ ] Documented in tethys README

title: CI fence: self-index oracle (zero Definite unused-imports findings on tethys itself)
description:
The PRD (tethys-l6nt, Testing Decisions) calls for a self-index oracle as a CI fence: tethys compiles warning-free, so any Definite-tier finding by unused-imports (and later dead-code) against tethys itself is by definition a false positive. No such CI job or integration test exists today (checked 2026-07-12 during tethys-3i35 planning: .github/workflows/ci.yml has no self-index step, tests/ has no self-index fence).

Shape: a CI step (or ignored-by-default integration test wired into CI) that runs 'tethys index' + 'tethys unused-imports --json' against the tethys checkout and fails on any Definite finding. Extend to each new analysis with a compiler-adjacent ground truth (visibility-tightening vs a pub-item audit, dead-code once shipped).

Until this lands, per-PR self-index checks are manual one-shot audits (tethys-3i35 runs one as its C11 verification).

title: Compute module_path for symbols
description:
The schema has a `module_path` column (indexed) but symbols are stored with empty strings. See `lib.rs:624` and `lib.rs:753`.

The existing `resolve_module_path` in `resolver.rs` computes **file paths**, not **module paths** (like `crate::storage::issue`). Need to compute the actual Rust module path from the file location.

This would enable queries like "find all symbols in module X".

title: fn-as-value: block-precise suppression (nested-fn leak, let_condition over-harvest)
description: Follow-up from PR #19 (tethys-ygjx) gemini review, findings #2/#3/#4 (verified; converge with design.md negative-space #4 'whole-function over-approximation, settled rationale'). collect_local_bindings (src/languages/rust.rs) over-approximates the suppression set two ways: (1) it descends into nested function_items, so an inner fn's param/let names leak into the outer fn's suppression set; (2) for let_condition it harvests the whole node incl. the value expr (e.g. 'maybe' in 'if let Some(y) = maybe'). Both only OVER-suppress (conservative, never fabricate refs) and do not change the measured 24/59 tethys split, but a genuine outer fn-as-value could rarely be missed. Fixes: return early on nested FUNCTION_ITEM in collect_bindings_recursive; move LET_CONDITION to the pattern-field arm. NB: doing so diverges from probe.py's whole-subtree logic — update the probe in lockstep. P4: refines a deliberately-conservative, approved design, not a defect.

title: Add rayon for parallel file parsing
description:
Use rayon to parallelize file parsing during indexing. Currently tethys processes files sequentially, using only one CPU core.

**Drift's pattern:**
```rust
// Parallel parsing with rayon
let results: Vec<_> = files
    .par_iter()
    .filter_map(|file| process_file(file).ok())
    .collect();
```

**Key considerations:**
- Parse in parallel, write to SQLite sequentially (SQLite doesn't like concurrent writes)
- Use MPSC channel to send parsed data to a background writer thread
- Batch SQLite writes (100 files per transaction) to amortize overhead
- Use atomic counters for thread-safe progress tracking

**Expected improvement:**
- 1,000 files: ~2-3s → ~0.5-1s
- 10,000 files: ~20-30s → ~3-5s
- Full CPU utilization instead of single core

**Implementation steps:**
1. Add `rayon = "1.10"` to Cargo.toml
2. Create `BatchWriter` struct with MPSC channel
3. Refactor `index_workspace` to use `par_iter()` for Pass 1
4. Keep Pass 2 (import resolution) and Pass 3 (LSP) sequential

title: Visibility tightening: C# parity (public types/members that could be internal)
description: C# analog of tethys-xoxq: public symbols used only within their own assembly are candidates for internal. Needs assembly attribution (arch_packages maps C# via directory/manifest source) and InternalsVisibleTo awareness. PRD tethys-l6nt Act 1 says both indexed languages get the same health signals.

title: Implement incremental index updates
description:
Currently `update()` in `lib.rs:2095-2104` re-indexes everything. The schema stores `mtime_ns` and `size_bytes` which could enable proper incremental updates:

1. Query files from DB
2. Compare mtime/size with filesystem
3. Only re-parse changed files
4. Remove deleted files from index

This would significantly improve performance for large codebases.

## 2026-07-12 pi-lens implementation review — design input

**Adopt hash-as-confirmation (their #202).** pi-lens's review-graph builder (clients/review-graph/builder.ts:354-411) treats mtime/size drift as a CANDIDATE signal only: drifted files are re-hashed (sha256), and an unchanged hash reuses the already-parsed results — so formatter no-ops, git checkout, and touch(1) never trigger re-parse. Degrades to mtime-only when no prior hash exists: "never a false reuse." Maps directly onto our existing classify logic (src/reindex.rs:202-223 compares mtime+size) — that comparison becomes the nomination tier; the hash becomes the confirmation tier. Depends on tethys-3l14 populating content_hash (dep edge added).

**Acceptance criterion to adopt: the equivalence property test.** k incremental updates over a mutation sequence must equal a from-scratch full rebuild, byte-identical index dump (pi-lens fences this in tests/clients/word-index-incremental.test.ts; their docs/word-index.md:80-83 states the property). This is the fence that makes an incremental path trustworthy, and it is the same discipline as our existing corpus-audit freeze checks (reindex idempotency byte-identical, .tethys-53iv/audit.md) — extend that fence from "reindex of unchanged tree" to "incremental over changed tree."

**Refuse-to-patch beats partial consistency.** When the metadata an incremental step needs is absent or from an older schema generation, refuse and fall back to a full rebuild rather than patching partially (pi-lens bails to full rebuild when its forward index is missing, word-index.ts:182-223). Cheap insurance for the first release of incremental.

**For the act-2 long-lived server (later, but record now):**
- Generation-stamped derived caches: any cache derived from the index (reverse-deps, coupling views materialized in memory) keys its validity to a build-generation carried ON the instance, not a global "latest build" slot — pi-lens hit real bugs from overlapping rebuilds clobbering the shared slot (builder.ts buildGeneration, their #459).
- Seq-laundering guard (their #348): never re-stamp carried-forward state with a fresh sequence number when merging partial state into a snapshot — stale data re-stamped fresh is a staleness forgery.
- Their seq fast-path guardrails if we add an event-driven path: cap the fast path at ~32 changed files (beyond that, full sweep is cheaper), and force a full re-verify periodically (theirs: every 5 minutes or every 20th fast-path build) as an external-edit safety valve.

Related: rivets-o3s8 already tracks the eager full-map load in the staleness sweep; tethys-ng1v proposes a reindex-unchanged perf trip-wire once this lands.
notes: Design constraint (from codebase-memory-mcp comparison, 2026-07-01): incremental via demote-and-rerun-Pass-2 is currently LOSSY. Pass 2 NULLs refs.reference_name on resolution (tethys-6rlu). When a changed file's symbols are deleted, inbound resolved refs from OTHER files must be demoted to unresolved and re-resolved — the bare name is recoverable from the symbol row inside the same transaction BEFORE the delete, but refs that originally carried a qualified path (e.g. crate::helper) cannot be reconstructed from symbols.name. A scoped Pass-2 re-run would therefore see weaker input than the original full pass, and the required fence — incremental(edit) produces a canonical dump byte-identical to full-rebuild(edited tree), reusing the .idxperf/probe-dump.py oracle — would diverge. Any incremental design must either retain the original reference_name (separate column; do NOT resurrect denormalizing onto reference_name — the 6rlu subtractive sweep showed the NULL semantics are load-bearing for panic-points and streaming file_deps) or snapshot demotion info at delete time. Reference pattern: codebase-memory-mcp pipeline_incremental.c snapshots cross-file inbound edges before delete+reinsert, then re-links.

title: lsp: Pass 3 queries rust-analyzer before workspace load completes (cold-workspace resolution silently empty)
description:
Found 2026-07-14 during the tethys-2d1x fix (PR #27); verified by direct observation, not just claimed.

## Defect

Pass 3 LSP resolution queries rust-analyzer immediately after `initialize`, but rust-analyzer loads the workspace asynchronously and returns empty results / `-32801 content modified` until the load finishes (~2s even on a tiny fixture). The comment at src/resolve.rs:807-809 claims rust-analyzer "indexes on startup and responds immediately, so no wait needed" — false. `wait_for_solution_load` (src/lsp/transport.rs:453) exists but matches the csharp-ls "Loading workspace" progress title and is gated on `language == Language::CSharp` (src/resolve.rs:810), so Rust gets no readiness wait at all.

## Observed

Cold small fixture: `LspCompletedSession { resolved_count: 0, unresolved_attempted: 1, error_count: 0 }` — the ref stays unresolved and no error is surfaced. Failure is invisible: strategy=lsp binds silently never happen on cold Rust workspaces.

## Consequence for the test suite

The existing LSP integration tests (tests/lsp_resolution.rs etc., e.g. `lsp_resolves_trait_method_call`) pass in ~0.07s via tree-sitter-resolved data alone — they never verify an LSP-contributed resolution, so this race is structurally invisible to them. The tethys-2d1x fence test had to pin at the `LspClient` seam (with its own readiness poll) instead of the natural "binds with strategy=lsp" pipeline shape for exactly this reason. Relevant to the nightly LSP job (tethys-xpc4).

## Fix shape (mechanism decision needed — NOT sweep-small)

Wait for rust-analyzer readiness before Pass 3 queries: either consume its work-done progress notifications (analogous to the csharp-ls path but with rust-analyzer progress tokens), or poll a probe request until it stops returning content-modified/empty. Natural red: a pipeline-level test asserting a ref binds with strategy=lsp on a cold fixture (red today).

## Blast radius

Today: --lsp opt-in resolution on Rust silently contributes nothing on cold workspaces. Blocks tethys-k543: Pass 3 re-verify with REBIND authority built on a racing query would inherit the race.

Provenance: discovered by the tethys-2d1x sweep subagent (PR #27), 2026-07-14.

title: Symbol binding is kind-blind (Pass-1 last-wins map; Pass-2 union/unique candidate sets): same-named symbols of different kinds collide
description: Two facets, one root cause: candidate sets for ref binding ignore symbol kind (only Macro is gated, resolve.rs:37-39). (1) Pass-1: src/db/files.rs:278 builds name_to_id with HashMap::insert (last-in-document-order wins), so a file declaring a method Count and a property Count binds ALL same-file refs named Count to whichever appears last. (2) Pass-2: the C# union arm and unique_workspace/same_crate arms decline on >=2 candidates, so adding a property named Foo in the same namespace as a method Foo flips previously-resolving Foo() calls to unresolved (conservative decline, but a behavior change). Pre-existing for class-vs-method collisions; tethys-xebx widens the surface by adding property/field/event/delegate symbols. Measured during xebx design on the Tethys.Results corpus: zero same-file name collisions between would-be member symbols and existing symbols; the xebx C11 audit diffs call/construct resolution pre/post to catch cross-file flips. Proper fix: kind-aware candidate filtering (call refs prefer invokables, member reads prefer data members), resolver semantics work adjacent to receiver-type precision (tethys-53iv). Falsifiers: same-file fixture (method+property collision; call binds by document order not kind); cross-file fixture (call resolving uniquely pre-property, declining post-property).

title: tethys: add format_uri_percent_encodes_unicode_unix to complete symmetric test matrix
description:
The format_uri test matrix has a Unix gap: format_uri_percent_encodes_unicode_as_utf8_bytes is #[cfg(windows)]-only. The Windows-side spaces test (format_uri_percent_encodes_spaces) has a Unix counterpart (format_uri_percent_encodes_spaces_unix), but the Unicode variant doesn't.

Without the Unix counterpart, a future refactor that accidentally moves percent_encode_path or PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET behind #[cfg(windows)] would silently regress Unix non-ASCII path support — CI on Linux/macOS runners wouldn't catch it.

Trivial add: ~5 lines mirroring the existing Windows test, asserting that /home/user/日本/foo.rs encodes to file:///home/user/%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC/foo.rs.

Surfaced by PR #64 (closed rivets-714v) — recognized post-merge that the symmetric matrix was incomplete.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Test format_uri_percent_encodes_unicode_unix added to crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs tests module
- [ ] Gated #[cfg(not(windows))]
- [ ] Asserts UTF-8 byte-level encoding of CJK characters on a Unix-shaped path
- [ ] cargo nextest run -p tethys passes on Linux and macOS
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-w3z7 (transport.rs format_uri hardening bundle); full details preserved here.

title: Implement LspClient with JSON-RPC transport
description:
Create `src/lsp/mod.rs` with:

- `LspClient` struct (process handle, stdin/stdout, request_id)
- `send_request<R: Request>()` - generic JSON-RPC request
- `read_response<T>()` - parse Content-Length header + JSON body
- `start()` - spawn process, do initialize handshake
- `shutdown()` - graceful shutdown

Use lsp-types for all protocol types, just implement the transport (~100 lines).

title: Functions defined inside macro invocations (proptest!, etc.) are not indexed as symbols
description: Discovered during tethys-s8hv. After MOD_ITEM recurses into inline module bodies, is_test reaches ~809 of ~842 source test fns. The residual gap is test functions DEFINED inside a macro invocation — e.g. `proptest! { fn normalize_path_is_idempotent(...) {...} }` at src/db/files.rs:841. tree-sitter parses these as macro_invocation, not function_item, so extract_symbols_recursive never sees them. Impact: such tests are absent as is_test roots (untested-code completeness) and their bodies' refs are unattached. Related to the macro-token gap in tethys-ygjx but distinct (function DEFINITIONS in macros, not value/token refs). Fix likely needs parsing the macro token stream for proptest's fn-shaped items, or a proptest-specific arm.

title: Rust refs inside macro invocations are invisible: .unwrap() in assert!/assert_eq! args produces no ref, panic-points under-reports
description: tree-sitter-rust parses macro invocation arguments as token_tree nodes, not expressions, so extract_references_recursive never sees call/method expressions inside macro args. Related: tethys-ygjx (closed, PR #19) shipped VALUE refs for macro-token-tree identifiers — but those are kind 'value' (dropped when unresolved, excluded from panic-points' reference_name filter), so the panic-points recall gap remains. Measured during the 53iv probe on tethys src/: grep finds 728 .unwrap()/.expect( sites, the refs table holds 661 reference_name-matching rows — the 68-site delta is macro-context calls (sampled: assert_eq!(result.expect(...)) at src/cargo.rs:572, chained .expect at src/batch_writer.rs:419). Consequence: panic-points structurally under-reports unwraps inside assert!/assert_eq!/format! etc. (mostly test code, not exclusively). Evidence: .tethys-53iv/probe3-output.txt + findings.md. Possible fix: re-parse token_tree contents as expressions, or count macro-context panic-name tokens into panic-points via the ygjx value-ref channel before the unresolved drop.

title: Snapshot and diff: track structural graph changes over time
description:
