Save lightweight snapshots of the graph and compare them to current state. Useful before/after refactors, in CI to audit what a PR added or removed structurally, and as a baseline for regression detection.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's snapshot save / snapshot diff / kirograph_diff MCP tool. Storage is just JSON arrays of node IDs and edge tuples; diff is set-difference, O(n) regardless of codebase size.

**Implementation:**
- Storage: .rivets/snapshots/{label}.json with serialized list of (qualified_name, kind) for nodes and (caller_qn, callee_qn, kind) for edges. JSON over native binary because they're small and human-readable.
- Default snapshot label is the timestamp; named labels (e.g. 'pre-refactor') are first-class.
- Diff is computed as two set differences: added = current - snapshot, removed = snapshot - current.
- Public API:
  - Tethys::save_snapshot(label) -> Result<SnapshotMeta>
  - Tethys::list_snapshots() -> Result<Vec<SnapshotMeta>>
  - Tethys::diff_snapshot(label) -> Result<SnapshotDiff>
- CLI:
  - tethys snapshot save [LABEL]
  - tethys snapshot list
  - tethys snapshot diff [LABEL] [--format full|summary|json]

**CI use case:**
gh pr create … then tethys snapshot save pre-pr; merge; tethys snapshot diff pre-pr to audit structural changes.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] save_snapshot, list_snapshots, diff_snapshot API methods
- [ ] CLI subcommands snapshot save / list / diff
- [ ] Snapshot file format documented (JSON schema in module-level docs)
- [ ] Diff returns added_symbols, removed_symbols, added_edges, removed_edges
- [ ] Default label is timestamp; named labels supported
- [ ] Round-trip test: save → diff against unchanged graph returns zero changes

title: lsp: positions sent as byte columns; UTF-16 never negotiated (wrong goto_definition on non-ASCII lines)
description:
Found 2026-07-12 during the pi-lens implementation review (verified against tethys source, not just claimed).

## Defect

LSP resolution sends tree-sitter BYTE column offsets as LSP positions, but the LSP default position encoding is UTF-16 code units, and tethys never negotiates. Any line containing non-ASCII text BEFORE the reference column (string literal with an em-dash or CJK text, a doc comment with an accented name) sends rust-analyzer a position pointing at the wrong character: goto_definition returns the wrong target or nothing.

## Evidence (tethys)

- src/languages/tree_sitter_utils.rs:35-38 — columns originate as node.start_position().column = BYTE offset (tree-sitter Point semantics), stored 1-indexed.
- src/resolve.rs:955-957 — passed to LSP with only saturating_sub(1); no conversion.
- src/lsp/transport.rs:153-159 — ClientCapabilities declares only window.workDoneProgress; general.positionEncodings (LSP 3.17) is absent, so servers assume the default UTF-16.
- Repo-wide grep for position_encoding/utf16/encode_utf16: zero matches (2026-07-12).

## Blast radius

- Today (--lsp opt-in): strategy=lsp binds on affected lines are silently wrong or lost. Failure is invisible — a null result just leaves the ref unresolved (no retry, no error).
- After tethys-k543 (Pass 3 re-verifies SPECULATIVE bindings with authority to REBIND): a wrong position could actively corrupt a correct speculative binding. This issue therefore BLOCKS k543 (dep edge added).

## Fix shape (reference: pi-lens clients/lsp/position-encoding.ts — isolated pure module, ~60 lines)

1. Declare general.positionEncodings in ClientCapabilities. For tethys prefer ["utf-8", "utf-16"]: our stored offsets ARE utf-8/byte offsets, so a server that accepts utf-8 (rust-analyzer does) makes conversion an identity no-op.
2. Read the server's chosen encoding from the initialize result; fall back to utf-16 when absent (spec default).
3. Convert OUTGOING positions only, per request: when the negotiated encoding is utf-16, take the line's text, slice the byte prefix at our stored column, re-measure as UTF-16 code units (s[..byte_col].encode_utf16().count()). Note this is the INVERSE of pi-lens's hot path (their stored offsets are UTF-16 JS string indexes so utf-16 is identity; ours is utf-8 identity) — do not copy their fast-path direction blindly.
4. Incoming ranges: currently immaterial — match-back is line-granular (find_symbol_at_line, src/db/symbols.rs:457-476, picks the lowest-column symbol on the def line). Leave a comment fence: if match-back ever becomes column-sensitive, incoming positions need the inverse conversion.
5. On a line-text read failure fall back to the raw offset rather than dropping the request (pi-lens behavior).

## Test shape

- Pure unit tests for the conversion fn (no server needed): ASCII line (identity), multibyte-before-column, multibyte-after-column (identity), emoji (surrogate pair: 4 utf-8 bytes = 2 utf-16 units).
- One ignored rust-analyzer integration case in tests/lsp_*.rs: fixture with a non-ASCII string literal before a method call on the same line; assert the ref binds with strategy=lsp (red today). Runs in the nightly LSP job (tethys-xpc4).

Provenance: pi-lens 2026-07-12 implementation review; their negotiation module is clients/lsp/position-encoding.ts, wire-in at clients/lsp/client.ts:1388-1408 (toWirePosition), capability advert at client.ts:395.
notes: Closed: Fixed: PR #27 (0b96251). Repro: lsp::encoding unit fences (utf16_col_shrinks_after_multibyte_prefix, utf16_col_counts_surrogate_pairs_for_emoji, negotiation_*) + ignored integration lsp_resolves_ref_after_non_ascii_text_on_same_line (nightly LSP job).

title: tethys: test format_uri returns Ok for nonexistent path (I/O-free seam)
description:
The path_to_uri / format_uri split exposes a contract that format_uri is I/O-free (its rustdoc says 'without performing any filesystem I/O'). The existing test path_to_uri_returns_invalid_path_for_nonexistent only verifies the wrapper still surfaces canonicalize errors — it doesn't pin down the seam itself.

Add a test asserting format_uri(Path::new(r'C:\does\not\exist\foo.rs')) returns Ok. If someone later 'fixes' format_uri by re-introducing a metadata() or canonicalize() call, that test catches the regression and forces them to explain.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (pr-test-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Test added to crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs#tests
- [ ] Asserts format_uri returns Ok for a deliberately-nonexistent path
- [ ] cfg-gated appropriately for Windows/Unix path shapes
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-w3z7 (transport.rs format_uri hardening bundle); full details preserved here.

title: PR #60 polish backlog: cleanup items deferred from review rounds
description:
Collection of low-priority cleanup items surfaced during PR #60's third review round. Each is real but represents diminishing returns relative to the work already done; bundle them for a future hygiene pass.

**Items:**

1. Remove the now-redundant ''suggestions_capped_at_five'' test in cli/coupling.rs — the new ''suggestions_capped_at_five_returns_first_five_by_input_order'' is a strict superset. Both currently exist and assert the same length on identical inputs.

2. Add an ''add_file'' helper to ''package_coupling_tests'' (it already exists in ''repopulate_architecture_tests''). The new sort-order test in particular repeats the inline ''upsert_file'' pattern 7 times.

3. Strengthen ''get_package_coupling_returns_err_for_corrupt_target_source'': currently asserts only ''is_err()''. Add assertions that the error is the expected variant (''Error::Internal'') and that the message contains the corrupt source value (''totally-bogus'') so a future change that drops diagnostic context would fail the test.

4. Corrupt-source tests use hardcoded package ''id=1,2,3'' and rely on ''PRAGMA ignore_check_constraints'' silently succeeding. Add a verification ''SELECT'' after the corrupt INSERT to confirm the bad row landed, so a silent PRAGMA failure produces a clear diagnostic at the injection point instead of a confusing downstream assertion mismatch. Optionally switch the inserts to use ''INSERT ... RETURNING id'' or query back ids rather than hardcoding.

5. The ''completed_path_writes_nothing_to_stdout'' assertion message embeds the ''rivets-tuph'' ticket reference. Move it to a ''// TODO(rivets-tuph)'' comment above the assertion so the failure message stays clean and the editorial intent stays out of test output.

6. Tighten the ''#[expect(clippy::cast_precision_loss, reason = ...)]'' annotation on ''CouplingMetrics::instability'' to explicitly say ''the annotated cast is ; the numerator uses  which is infallible widening, exempt from the lint.''

7. Add a reciprocal cross-reference: ''Package.name'' field doc should point at ''PackageId'' (''recommended stable identity; see [] for why the numeric id is unsuitable for that purpose''). Currently the ''PackageId → name'' direction is documented but not the reverse.

8. The ''none_writes_nothing'' assertion is bare ''assert!(buf.is_empty())'' with no contract message. Add an explanation so a failure tells a future maintainer what the ''None'' arm represents.

Discovered during third-round PR #60 review (the 6 specialist reviewers ran on commits a4949bb..HEAD).

title: Pre-computed call graph edges table
description:
Store explicit caller→callee edges in a dedicated table for faster graph queries.

**Current approach:**
- `get_callers` joins references → symbols → files
- Complex query with multiple joins
- Re-computed on every query

**Drift's approach:**
- Dedicated `call_edges` table: `(caller_id, callee_id, call_count)`
- Pre-computed during indexing
- Simple lookups for callers/callees

**Proposed schema:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE call_edges (
    caller_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    callee_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    call_count INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    PRIMARY KEY (caller_symbol_id, callee_symbol_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (caller_symbol_id) REFERENCES symbols(id),
    FOREIGN KEY (callee_symbol_id) REFERENCES symbols(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_call_edges_callee ON call_edges(callee_symbol_id);
```

**Benefits:**
- O(1) lookup for "who calls X?" instead of join-heavy query
- Enables efficient reachability analysis
- Pre-computed call counts for hotspot detection

**Trade-off:**
- Slightly larger database
- Need to update edges during incremental re-index

title: Resolver declines single-segment relative import paths: pub use inner::item resolves only when the name is workspace-unique
description:
Import-path resolution declines bare single-segment relative module paths (source_module='inner' from use inner::item within src/lib.rs) — documented in tests/idxperf_golden.rs as 'the resolver declines the bare single-segment util import path'. Refs whose name matches such an import then fall through to the unscoped unique-name fallback: they resolve when the name is workspace-unique and stay unresolved otherwise.

Surfaced by the tethys-v1w8 slice-3 collision fence: pub use inner::helper (the MOST idiomatic re-export shape — parent re-exporting a child module item by relative path) plus a same-named decoy fn in another file leaves BOTH the reexport ref and a same-file bare call unresolved, while a crate::inner::helper-anchored import resolves fine. Parity between reexport refs and bare usage holds exactly (v1w8 C2) — the gap is upstream in path resolution and affects both equally.

Impact: (a) callers/impact under-count for non-unique names imported via bare relative paths; (b) dead-code residual false positive: a re-exported symbol whose name is not workspace-unique and whose pub use is bare-relative keeps zero inbound refs even after v1w8.

Likely fix shape: resolve single-segment relative paths against the importing file's own module directory (src/lib.rs + 'inner' -> src/inner.rs; src/a/mod-file + 'inner' -> src/a/inner.rs), i.e. the same semantics rustc gives a use path's leading in-crate segment. Interacts with tethys-9z7i (the current unique-name fallback binding would carry a 'speculative' band; this fix upgrades the strategy to path-directed).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: pub use inner::helper with a same-named decoy in another file resolves to inner.rs's helper (path-directed, not unique-fallback)
- [ ] Same fixture: bare body call via use inner::helper resolves identically (parity with v1w8 C2 fence)
- [ ] Existing anchored-path (crate::/self::) and multi-segment resolution unchanged
- [ ] tests/reexport_refs.rs collision fence expectations updated (it pins the decline today with a TRIPWIRE note)

title: Nested cfg_attr not recursively unwrapped: #[cfg_attr(a, cfg_attr(b, deprecated))]
description: cfg_attr_wrapped_attributes (PR #15) unwraps one level: the inner cfg_attr row is emitted but its own payload is not expanded, so a doubly-conditional attribute stays invisible to attribute-keyed analyses. Fix: make the unwrapping recursive (~10 lines + fence). Convergent flags: the tethys-n7nf fix agent's report AND gemini's review comment on PR #15. Exotic input shape in practice.

title: Implement depth-limited transitive analysis
description:
The `--depth` flag in `tethys impact` is accepted but ignored (with a warning). See `cli/impact.rs:20-27`.

Should limit how many levels of indirection to follow in transitive dependency analysis. Current behavior is full graph traversal.
notes: Closed: Already implemented: --depth was wired through tethys impact in PR #57 (verified at src/cli/impact.rs:16,24 — depth passed to get_symbol_impact/get_impact). Remaining follow-ups tracked in tethys-w0qw (--depth 0 semantics) and tethys-3yxn (callers --transitive parity).

title: Parse Cargo.toml to detect actual crate root
description:
The code currently hardcodes `workspace_root/src/` as the crate root in `lib.rs:1016` and `lib.rs:1443`. This breaks for:
- Workspaces with custom `[lib].path` in Cargo.toml
- Binary crates vs library crates
- Multi-crate workspaces

Should parse Cargo.toml to find the actual crate entry point.

title: Unify resolve_csharp_dependencies namespace-map mechanism with the ModuleResolver seam
description: indexing.rs resolve_csharp_dependencies is a pre-existing post-pass that re-parses C# files, joins using-directive segments into namespace names, and resolves file deps via a namespace->files map — a second C# resolution mechanism living outside the ModuleResolver seam (flagged by PR #1 review; the join now routes through join_import() but the mechanism itself remains separate). When implementing tethys-jwf9 (C# namespace->file resolution), fold this mechanism into CSharpModuleResolver (e.g. the resolver owns the namespace map as its anchor/context) so C# has one resolution path, then delete the post-pass.
notes: Closed by the csharp-ns loop (branch csharp-ns): CSharpModuleResolver resolves plain using-directives via the namespace map (types-only, unique-or-decline); the resolve_csharp_dependencies post-pass is deleted with C# file deps derived from resolved refs under L2 semantics. Audit trail in .csharp-ns/.

title: Update get_callers/get_symbol_impact to use resolved refs
description:
The graph operations should now pick up cross-file references since they're stored with proper symbol_id after Pass 2.

Verify that:
- `get_callers()` returns callers from other files
- `get_symbol_impact()` shows transitive dependents across files

title: Consolidate top-level comma splitters (db::deprecated + languages::rust siblings)
description: Two documented sibling implementations of split-on-top-level-commas: db/deprecated.rs (string-aware incl. verbatim/raw handling from PR #10) and languages/rust.rs (string+bracket-depth aware, added for cfg_attr unwrapping in PR #15, cross-referenced comment). Consolidate into one pub(crate) helper; while there, both mis-split on a comma inside a char literal (',') and neither handles r#".."# raw strings (documented degradation).

title: Implement parent_symbol_id for nested symbol tracking
description: Two TODO sites in lib.rs (lines 691, 823) pass None for parent_symbol_id. Implementing this would enable tracking nested symbols (methods within impl blocks, nested functions) for more precise dependency graphs.
notes: Closed: Duplicate of rivets-aay4 (older, describes the same parent_symbol_id TODOs in lib.rs).

title: tethys: implement proper incremental update and staleness check
description: CodeIndex::update() currently re-indexes everything instead of doing a proper incremental update. CodeIndex::needs_update() always returns true. Implement proper staleness detection and incremental re-indexing.
notes: Closed: Redundant union of tethys-q8qw (incremental update in CodeIndex::update) and tethys-gkt2 (needs_update staleness check, in_progress) — both remain open and together cover this issue's full scope.

title: tethys: compute and store content hash for indexed files
description: Content hash is currently passed as None when indexing files (in both lib.rs and batch_writer.rs). Implement content hash computation to enable cache invalidation based on file content rather than mtime alone.
notes: Closed: Duplicate of tethys-3l14 (same content_hash-always-None TODOs in lib.rs/batch_writer.rs; tethys-e7fp was previously closed as a dup of 3l14 for the same reason).

title: Tethys: Codebase Intelligence Engine
description:
Improvements inspired by analyzing the Drift codebase. Drift is a mature codebase intelligence platform that handles 10K+ file codebases efficiently.

Key patterns from Drift:
- Rayon for parallel file parsing
- Streaming SQLite writes via MPSC channel
- Pre-computed call graph edges
- MCP server integration for AI agents
- Reachability analysis for security auditing
- Test topology for CI integration

These improvements would make tethys production-ready for large codebases.

title: fn-as-value: extend value-position coverage (struct field init, assignment RHS, tuple/array)
description: Follow-up from PR #19 (tethys-ygjx) gemini review, finding #1 (verified valid). value_position_ref (src/languages/rust.rs) emits Value refs only for identifiers in ARGUMENTS / RETURN_EXPRESSION / let-value position. A bare in-crate free fn can also sit in: struct field initializer (field_initializer 'value' field), assignment RHS (assignment_expression 'right'), and tuple/array elements (tuple_expression, array_expression). These were out of scope for ygjx (AC #1 named only iter.map(foo) / let g = foo;, both covered) and unmeasured for noise. Recall enhancement: the existing scope-guard + drop-unresolved-value-refs already protect precision, so extending the match is low-risk once the per-position noise is measured (reuse .tethys-ygjx/probe.py). Add fixtures per new position.

title: tethys: expand file_deps test coverage (rename, target deletion, DB-unit, rebuild idempotency)
description:
Surfaced by pr-test-analyzer during multi-agent review of PR #65. The 2 regression tests added in rivets-lcb6 fence the specific reported bug well, but four adjacent code paths are uncovered.

Tests to add (in crates/tethys/tests/file_deps_idempotency.rs unless noted):

1. file_deps_swapped_when_use_target_changes (rating 8 / high). Replace 'use crate_target::Widget' with 'use crate_target_alt::Gadget' in the fixture. Re-index. Assert the edge to crate_target is GONE and the edge to crate_target_alt EXISTS — check by exact FileId lookup, not just row count (counts could net to zero coincidentally and hide the bug).

2. clear_all_file_deps unit test (rating 6 / medium). crates/tethys/src/db/file_deps.rs has no #[cfg(test)] mod tests block. clear_all_file_deps is pub and per CLAUDE.md 'every public method needs tests' (rust-best-practices Rule 39). Add: insert 2 deps via insert_file_dependency, call clear_all_file_deps, assert get_file_dependencies returns [] for both source FileIds. Catches SQL typos / schema renames the integration tests would miss.

3. file_deps_target_file_deletion_removes_edge (rating 8 / high but DEFERRED to rivets-dhxo). Delete crate_target/src/lib.rs from disk, re-index, assert no edges reference the orphan file. This test would currently FAIL in streaming mode — that's the whole point. Defer landing this until rivets-dhxo is fixed so it doesn't break CI. Cross-reference here so the regression-fence test gets added alongside the fix.

4. rebuild_with_options idempotency (rating 7 / medium). The design doc notes rebuild is 'correct by accident' because db.reset() wipes the table first. Add a #[case::rebuild] variant or separate test calling rebuild_with_options(IndexOptions::default()) twice and assert FileDepsSnapshot stability. Defends against someone 'optimizing' rebuild to skip the reset later.

5. (Suggestion, rating 4) file with 2 uses of same target → remove one → row remains, ref_count drops. Probes the multi-ref-per-edge code path the existing tests don't exercise.

Source: PR #65 multi-agent review aggregate (pr-test-analyzer findings 1-5).
acceptance_criteria:
* file_deps_swapped_when_use_target_changes added and passing
* clear_all_file_deps unit test added to db/file_deps.rs #[cfg(test)] mod tests
* rebuild_with_options idempotency test added (as #[case::rebuild] or standalone)
* (deferred) file_deps_target_file_deletion_removes_edge added once rivets-dhxo lands

title: C# member reads: shapes beyond plain member_access_expression are invisible (implicit-this, object initializers, ?., indexers)
description: tethys-xebx adds refs for plain member_access_expression reads (x.Data, per access level). Four production-reachable C# read shapes remain invisible because they are different grammar nodes: (1) implicit-this bare-identifier reads inside the declaring class (var v = Data;) — needs a scope-guarded suppression pass like Rust fn-as-value (tethys-ygjx) to avoid local-variable false positives; (2) object-initializer assignments (new Foo { Data = 1 } — identifier inside initializer_expression); (3) null-conditional access (obj?.Data — conditional_access_expression + member_binding_expression, a distinct node family); (4) indexer_declaration members (this[int i]) which have no simple name. Consequence: [Obsolete] reader sites of these shapes are missing from deprecated-callers. Evidence: .tethys-xebx/findings.md (probe1 walked the grammar; corpus contained none of these shapes reading the deprecated property). Also note nameof(x.Data) IS emitted by xebx as a read even though the C# compiler suppresses CS0618 there — accepted minor over-report, revisit here if it bothers anyone.

title: C# parser doesn't extract nested types (classes, records, structs)
description:
The C# parser's `extract_class_members` function only extracts methods and constructors from type declarations. It does NOT recurse into nested type declarations (classes, records, structs, interfaces).

**Impact**: References like `Distribution.Percentage` can't be resolved because `Percentage` (a nested record) is never indexed.

**Root cause**: `extract_class_members()` in `csharp.rs:663-696` only handles `METHOD_DECLARATION` and `CONSTRUCTOR_DECLARATION`. It should also handle `CLASS_DECLARATION`, `RECORD_DECLARATION`, `STRUCT_DECLARATION`, `INTERFACE_DECLARATION`.

**Test added**: `extracts_nested_types` test in `csharp.rs` (currently failing) demonstrates the issue.

**Example**:
```csharp
public abstract record Distribution
{
    public sealed record Percentage : Distribution { }  // NOT extracted
    public sealed record FixedAmount : Distribution { } // NOT extracted
}
```

Only `Distribution` is extracted, not the nested `Percentage` or `FixedAmount` types.

title: Auto-sync via dirty-marker + deferred flush
description:
Add a low-overhead pattern for keeping the tethys index fresh during active editing: file-save writes a tiny dirty marker (cheap, no parse), and the actual re-sync runs at session boundaries (e.g. agent stop, pre-prompt).

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's hook system. The pattern avoids per-keystroke overhead while keeping the graph fresh enough that AI assistants always see current code structure.

**Two-phase flow:**

Phase 1 — mark dirty (cheap):
- A new tethys mark-dirty subcommand writes <workspace>/.rivets/index/dirty.txt (one path per line, append-only).
- O(1) — no parsing, no SQL writes, suitable for per-save invocation from an editor or hook.

Phase 2 — sync if dirty (deferred):
- A new tethys sync-if-dirty subcommand reads dirty.txt, re-indexes only the listed files (incremental), and clears the marker.
- Suitable for invocation at agent stop, pre-prompt, or other session boundaries.

**Hook integration:**
- Claude Code: PostToolUse hook on Edit/Write writes via mark-dirty; Stop hook runs sync-if-dirty.
- Editors: file-save hooks invoke mark-dirty.
- CI: a pre-test step runs sync-if-dirty to ensure the index reflects HEAD.

**Why this depends on rivets-q8qw and rivets-3l14:**
- rivets-q8qw (incremental updates): without it, the sync re-indexes the whole workspace, defeating the deferred-flush optimization.
- rivets-3l14 (content hash): mtime alone is not a reliable change indicator (touch updates mtime without changing content). Content hash makes ''actually changed'' the trigger.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::mark_dirty(paths: &[Path]) -> Result<()>
- Tethys::sync_if_dirty() -> Result<IndexUpdate>
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] mark_dirty and sync_if_dirty API methods
- [ ] CLI subcommands tethys mark-dirty PATH... and tethys sync-if-dirty [--quiet]
- [ ] Dirty marker format documented (newline-separated relative paths, dedup on read)
- [ ] sync-if-dirty is a no-op when marker is absent or empty
- [ ] Race-safe: mark-dirty appends with lock; sync-if-dirty reads-then-truncates atomically
- [ ] Example Claude Code hook config in tethys README
- [ ] Tests cover: empty marker, deleted file in marker, concurrent mark + sync

title: Implement Pass 2: resolve references against symbol DB
description:
After all files are indexed, run a second pass to resolve unresolved references:

1. Query all references with symbol_id = NULL
2. For each, look up import context from imports table
3. Match reference name against symbols from imported modules
4. Update reference with resolved symbol_id

Resolution priority:
1. Same-file lookup
2. Explicit import lookup
3. Qualified path resolution
4. Glob import search
5. Leave unresolved (LSP candidate)

title: Audit every shipped analysis for real consumers (widened from coupling epic)
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Further Notes: the architecture/coupling feature is substantially shipped but its epic remains open.

## What to build

Not code — a reconciliation pass. tethys-byie (architecture analysis: packages + Ca/Ce/instability coupling metrics) predates PR #60, which shipped the coupling CLI and its schema. Walk byie acceptance criteria one by one and mark each shipped (with evidence: command exists, test exists, README section exists) or not shipped.

Outcome, one of:
- All criteria shipped or re-homed: close byie with an audit-trail reason.
- A real remainder exists (e.g. a planned CLI subcommand or docs that never landed): narrow byie to exactly that remainder, or close it and file the remainder as a fresh, small issue.

The MCP-tool criteria inside byie belong to Act 2 — re-home them to tethys-o4re rather than leaving them as Act 1 residue. Do not modify the scope PRD itself.

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately.

## Widened scope (2026-07-12, grill session — pi-lens 'built but not self-consumed' lesson)

Beyond the coupling epic: audit EVERY shipped analysis — coupling, cycles, panic-points, visibility-tightening, deprecated-callers, unused-imports, search, stats — for an actual consumer (CI recipe, dogfood step, documented workflow, external tool). Output: (1) a short dated report in docs/ listing per-analysis verdicts (consumed / idle inventory), (2) follow-up rivets issues per idle analysis: promote (wire a consumer), document, or candidate-for-removal. One-shot audit; repeat only when it earns it — no standing-doc ritual (the PRD owns forward triage; this owns the backward 'does anything use what we shipped' question the PRD never asked).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Every byie acceptance criterion classified: shipped (with evidence) / re-homed / genuine remainder
- [ ] MCP-related criteria re-homed to tethys-o4re
- [ ] byie closed with an audit-trail reason, or narrowed to only the verified remainder
- [ ] No new duplicate issues created — search the tracker before filing any remainder

title: use-list alias members dropped: use m::{A, B as C} loses B from imports and refs
description:
collect_use_list_names (Rust use-declaration parsing) handles identifier and scoped_identifier children of a use_list but has NO use_as_clause arm, so a group member with an alias — use m::{A, B as C} — drops B entirely: no imports row, and (post tethys-v1w8) no reexport ref when the statement is pub use.

Impact today: the import of B is invisible to unused-imports, file_deps corroboration, and Pass-2 explicit-import resolution (a body use of C resolves how?  — verify while fixing). Impact post-v1w8: a group-aliased re-export target keeps zero inbound refs (dead-code false positive persists for exactly this shape).

Top-level alias (use m::B as C;) is handled correctly by parse_use_as_clause — this is only the in-group form.

Sibling of tethys-pdea (nested-group intermediate segment drop) — same collect_use_list_names neighborhood, distinct symptom (pdea keeps the name with a wrong path; this drops the name entirely).

Discovered during tethys-v1w8 slice 2 while establishing the parity baseline for reexport-ref emission; v1w8 deliberately mirrors current parse behavior rather than fixing this (parity posture).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] use m::{A, B as C} yields an imports row for B (alias C recorded)
- [ ] pub use m::{A, B as C} yields a reexport ref for B (original name, per v1w8 C3)
- [ ] Nested and multi-alias groups covered by fixture
- [ ] Existing non-alias group parsing unchanged (regression)

title: Incrementally add #[errors] doc to public Tethys methods
description: The entire impl Tethys block has #[expect(clippy::missing_errors_doc)]. As the API stabilizes, chip away at this by documenting errors on individual methods and narrowing the expect scope. Flagged in PR #55 review.

title: tethys: --rebuild flag fails on schema changes
description:
When the database schema changes (e.g., adding new columns like `is_test`),
running `tethys index --rebuild` USED TO fail because CREATE TABLE IF NOT
EXISTS doesn't add columns and new indexes on missing columns error.

PROBE EVIDENCE (2026-07-04, .tethys-9z7i/probe1.sh, tethys-9z7i slice 2):
the --rebuild path is FIXED — Index::reset() now deletes the db file +
WAL/SHM sidecars and reopens fresh (canary column vanished after
--rebuild). Residual gap, still open: a NON-rebuild open of an old-schema
DB (any query command, plain index) still hits raw 'no such column'
errors with no guidance — AC3 ('clear user feedback when schema is
incompatible') is unmet. tethys-9z7i slice 2 adds refs.strategy and will
need at least an idempotent ALTER for that column; whether a general
version-check mechanism lands there or here is a slice-2 design decision.

Original error observed:
  error: database error: no such column: is_test in
  CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_symbols_is_test ON symbols(is_test)
design:
Options to consider:

1. **Delete and recreate** (simplest): Have `--rebuild` delete the database file entirely before creating a fresh one. This is semantically correct since "rebuild" implies starting fresh.

2. **Schema migration**: Add version tracking and migration logic to handle schema evolution. More complex but preserves data when possible.

3. **Hybrid**: Check schema version on open, delete and recreate if incompatible.

Recommended: Option 1 for now (delete on rebuild), with option 3 as future enhancement.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `tethys index --rebuild` works correctly after schema changes
- [ ] No manual deletion of database required
- [ ] Clear user feedback when schema is incompatible
notes: Closed: Fixed across reset() (delete-on-rebuild, shipped earlier) and PR #13 (7f7fda5): Index::open now rejects outdated schemas with a clear run---rebuild error (AC3), and index --rebuild clears db files pre-open so the remedy is always reachable (fence: rebuild_recovers_from_outdated_schema). Probe evidence in .tethys-9z7i/probe1.sh.

title: BatchWriter error-handling hardening: surface failures instead of log-and-continue (consolidates 3 issues)
description:
Consolidated design decision: how does BatchWriter surface failure? Three previously separate tickets each covered one facet and risked three inconsistent answers if fixed independently. Bundles tethys-24av, tethys-ghip, tethys-kivr (all closed as consolidated-into-this; full details remain readable there via rivets show).

Facets:
1. (was tethys-24av) WriteStats only counts successes. Add failed_files (path + error message) so failures survive into the final result instead of being lost after a log line.
2. (was tethys-ghip) write_batch() catches ALL database errors as warnings and continues — systemic failures (disk full, DB corruption) produce thousands of warnings instead of one clear abort. Add a consecutive-failure threshold (e.g. MAX_CONSECUTIVE_FAILURES = 5) that converts systemic failure into a hard error.
3. (was tethys-kivr) BatchWriter::send() silently drops data when the channel is disconnected (background writer thread crashed), only logging. Return Result so callers can detect data loss.

These should be designed together: the threshold (2) feeds the stats (1), and the send() contract (3) determines who observes the abort. One PR, one error-propagation story.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] WriteStats carries failed_files with per-file error context
- [ ] Consecutive-failure threshold aborts write_batch on systemic errors with a single clear error
- [ ] send() returns Result; callers handle or propagate channel disconnect
- [ ] Tests: injected write failure appears in WriteStats; threshold aborts; disconnected channel surfaces as Err
- [ ] Error-propagation behavior documented on BatchWriter

title: consistency fences: strategy-to-band exhaustiveness + per-language corpus fixtures
description:
Two "membership enforced by a check, not by memory" fences (pi-lens lesson; grill session 2026-07-12). Scope deliberately excludes what the compiler already enforces (Language/strategy exhaustive matches).

1. Strategy-to-band exhaustiveness: every ResolutionStrategy variant must map to a band in the schema view CASE (the SQL string is invisible to the compiler). PROBE FIRST: src/types.rs:1467 area already has a variant-to-string mapping test — establish what is and is not covered, then absorb or extend rather than duplicate.
2. Per-language corpus fixtures: every Language enum variant must have at least one corpus fixture exercised by tests/ — a language wired into the enum but without fixtures passes compile and review silently.

Both are test-only; one small PR.

title: Track failed files in WriteStats for debugging
description:
Currently `WriteStats` only tracks successful writes. When files fail to write, that information is logged but lost from the final result.

**Current struct:**
```rust
pub struct WriteStats {
    pub files_written: usize,
    pub symbols_written: usize,
    pub references_written: usize,
    pub batches_committed: usize,
}
```

**Suggested addition:**
```rust
pub struct WriteStats {
    // ... existing fields ...
    /// Files that failed to write with their error messages
    pub failed_files: Vec<(PathBuf, String)>,
}
```

**Benefits:**
- Users can see which files need attention without grepping logs
- Enables retry logic for failed files
- Makes partial success explicit rather than hidden

**Trade-off:** Increases memory usage if many files fail (unlikely in practice).
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-rddn (BatchWriter error-handling bundle); the three facets share one design decision and should ship as one PR.

title: Discriminated NotFound variants in tethys::Error (e.g., PackageNotFound)
description:
Currently tethys::Error::NotFound(String) carries an ad-hoc payload format like 'package: foo', 'file: bar', 'symbol: baz'. The string content is a de facto protocol for distinguishing what was not found, but nothing in the type enforces or documents it.

This becomes a problem when rivets-mcp adds a tethys_coupling tool. If MCP needs to react differently to 'package not found' vs 'file not found' (e.g., suggest re-indexing for the former, suggest checking the path for the latter), it has to parse the string payload — a brittle implicit contract.

**Fix:** Introduce discriminated variants on tethys::Error:
- PackageNotFound(String)
- (existing NotFound(String) stays as catch-all for legacy callers)

Update get_package_coupling and cli/coupling.rs::run_detail_to to use PackageNotFound.

**Why deferred:** Low urgency today because (a) no MCP consumer of get_package_coupling exists yet, (b) the string format isn't being parsed anywhere, (c) main.rs renders both variants the same way. The right time to fix is just before rivets-o4re wires up tethys_coupling.

Discovered during the durability review pass on PR #60.

title: seam fence: src/cli must not import crate::db or hold &Index
description:
Lock in the adapter seam before Act 2 opens a second adapter (pi-lens lesson: their documented-but-unfenced seam eroded into a god-adapter; grill session 2026-07-12).

Verified 2026-07-12: src/cli/* currently has ZERO matches for use crate::db / db::Index / &Index — every command goes through the Tethys facade. This issue is a test-only fence in the style of tests/seam_lint.rs (include_str! + needle asserts, TDD-inversion verified) so the clean state cannot silently regress before the MCP adapter (tethys-o4re) lands and extends the same fence to the mcp module.

title: tethys: define and document --depth 0 semantics for impact analysis
description:
After PR #57, `tethys impact --depth N` is wired through. `--depth 0` is currently undefined behavior: it would call `get_transitive_dependents(file_id, Some(0))` which depends on whatever the SQLite recursive CTE does at depth 0 (likely returns nothing).

Decide and document the contract: either treat 0 as 'direct dependents only and no transitive search', or reject it at the CLI layer with a friendly error. Add a test pinning the behavior either way.

title: Path queries: shortest path between two symbols
description:
Find the shortest connection between any two symbols, traversing all edge kinds (calls, refs, imports). Answers ''how is X connected to Y?'' for code review and impact analysis.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_path tool and CLI command. Tethys already has get_dependency_chain for files; the same BFS over the union of edge kinds gives symbol-to-symbol path-finding.

**Implementation:**
- BFS shortest-path search over the union of call_edges and refs (grouped by symbol_id, in_symbol_id).
- Public API:
  - Tethys::get_symbol_path(from: &str, to: &str) -> Result<Option<Vec<PathStep>>>
  - PathStep { symbol, edge_kind, line, file }
- CLI: tethys path <FROM> <TO> [--format text|json]
- Resolve symbol names with the same fuzzy matcher as tethys search; prefer real-symbol matches over file/import nodes.
- Output shows each hop with file:line and the edge kind used.

**Edge cases:**
- No path exists → return None / clear CLI message.
- Symbol not found → standard NotFound error.
- Self-path (from == to) → empty path with clear semantics.

**Test plan:**
Build a fixture workspace with three modules a → b → c plus an unrelated d, and verify path(a, c) returns the two-hop path while path(a, d) returns None.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] get_symbol_path() public API method
- [ ] CLI subcommand tethys path FROM TO
- [ ] BFS over union of call_edges and refs
- [ ] Returns each hop with file:line and edge kind
- [ ] Disconnected symbols return None / friendly CLI message
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_path (sibling rivets-o4re)
- [ ] Tests cover: connected, disconnected, self-path, fuzzy name resolution

title: Add error threshold to BatchWriter.write_batch() for systemic failures
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-rddn (BatchWriter error-handling bundle); the three facets share one design decision and should ship as one PR.

title: Pass 2: self-crate qualified call crate::X() does not resolve to in-crate X
description:
A qualified call through the crate root, crate::helper(), to an in-crate free function helper (defined in the crate root lib.rs) is NOT resolved in Pass 2: the ref stays unresolved (symbol_id NULL) with reference_name='crate::helper'.

Contrast (both work): a BARE helper() via 'use crate::helper;' resolves; and a submodule-qualified helper::do_thing_q() resolves (see tests/pass2_qualified_paths.rs::submodule_qualified_call_resolves). So the gap is specifically the leading 'crate' segment / crate-root absolute path — resolve.rs::qualified_module_fallback + resolver::resolve_module_path do not map 'crate' to the crate root.

Repro:
  src/lib.rs:  pub mod b; pub fn helper() {}
  src/b.rs:    use crate::helper; pub fn use_it() { helper(); crate::helper(); }
  After index:
    SELECT symbol_id, reference_name FROM refs r JOIN files f ON r.file_id=f.id
      WHERE f.path LIKE '%b.rs';
    -> bare helper() : symbol_id set (resolved)
    -> crate::helper(): symbol_id NULL, reference_name='crate::helper' (UNRESOLVED)

Impact: callers/impact/reachability under-count symbols invoked via crate:: paths. Also surfaced in tethys-6rlu: the refs_named view keys this unresolved ref by 'crate::helper', so a bare-name query (WHERE name='helper') misses it. tethys-6rlu documents that as a limitation; resolving crate:: here would remove it (the view then keys the ref by symbols.name='helper', no view change needed).
acceptance_criteria:
* After indexing the repro, the crate::helper() ref resolves: symbol_id points to the in-crate helper symbol and reference_name is NULL.
* tethys callers helper lists the crate::helper() call site.
* Existing submodule-qualified and bare-call resolution unchanged (regression).
* A fixture pins crate::X() (X in crate root) resolving.
notes: Closed: Fixed in PR #24 (merge b9699c7): bare crate paths resolve to the compiler-faithful crate-root file via bare_crate_root_file; all ACs fenced (tests/pass2_crate_root_paths.rs, callers AC, monotonicity audit 0 removed/0 target changes).

title: Streaming dependency corroboration skips first-path-segment splitting (batch/streaming parity gap)
description: compute_dependencies_from_stored builds its refs-name set from raw stored reference_name strings (qualified, e.g. 'Index::open') with no first-path-segment splitting, unlike batch mode's referenced_names (the L2 invariant owner in languages/common.rs). An import used only via qualified paths may corroborate a file_dep in batch mode but not streaming. Found by the tethys-sp24 fix agent; pre-existing, not introduced by PR #17.

title: tethys: rename format_uri to absolute_path_to_uri
description:
format_uri's rustdoc says 'caller must pass an absolute, canonicalized path' — that precondition lives only in prose. A CanonicalPath newtype would be overkill for two private callsites, but the rename converts a hidden precondition into a name a reviewer can't miss.

Trivial: rename the fn, update the one caller in path_to_uri, update the five format_uri_* test names if desired (or leave them as the shorter form).

If the function ever goes pub(crate), revisit with CanonicalPath. Defer until that day.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (type-design-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Rename fn format_uri -> fn absolute_path_to_uri in crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs
- [ ] Update caller in path_to_uri
- [ ] Update doc comments referencing the old name
- [ ] cargo nextest run -p tethys passes
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-w3z7 (transport.rs format_uri hardening bundle); full details preserved here.

title: Pass 3 LSP re-verifies speculative-band resolutions, not just unresolved refs
description:
Today Pass 3 LSP only fetches refs Pass 2 DECLINED (symbol_id IS NULL). Confidently-wrong Pass-2 bindings — the tethys-53iv phantom-edge class — are therefore structurally invisible to rust-analyzer, the one component with receiver-type information that could correct them.

Once resolution provenance bands exist (blocking epic), widen the Pass 3 candidate set from "unresolved" to "unresolved UNION speculative-band". Match-back is position-based (goto_definition at the ref location -> find_symbol_at_line), so this does NOT depend on reference_name retention.

Policy decisions to settle in this issue before implementation:
- LSP disagrees with the speculative binding -> rebind symbol_id and upgrade band to lsp.
- LSP confirms -> upgrade band in place (binding unchanged).
- LSP timeout/error -> leave the speculative binding untouched (counted and capped, as today; never demote on LSP failure).
- Scoping/volume: per-request timeout is 60s, so re-verifying every speculative ref may be slow on large crates. Likely restrict to method-call refs (the 53iv class) and/or cap per run. Decide and document.

Still opt-in behind --lsp; the default path is unchanged (provenance epic handles labeling; 53iv handles the default-path fix).

CBM analog from the 2026-07-01 comparison: their type-aware resolver arbitrates against the textual registry with a 0.6 confidence floor, CALLS edges only — i.e., the type-aware component corrects the textual one rather than merely filling its gaps. Ours is a real language server, so its verdicts deserve at least that much authority.

## 2026-07-12 pi-lens implementation review — prerequisites and mechanics

**New hard blocker: tethys-2d1x (UTF-16 position encoding).** Pass 3 currently sends tree-sitter BYTE columns where servers expect UTF-16 units (src/resolve.rs:955-957, no negotiation anywhere). Giving Pass 3 authority to REBIND speculative bindings while positions can be wrong on non-ASCII lines would corrupt correct binds. Fix positions first; dep edge added.

**The readiness assumption is wrong — delete it.** src/resolve.rs:803-804 claims "rust-analyzer indexes on startup and responds immediately, so no wait needed." pi-lens's production experience contradicts this: rust-analyzer returns EMPTY first answers while still indexing. Their compensations (clients/lsp/server-strategies.ts:121-258): a pull-retry budget (500ms) specifically for the empty-first-answer-while-indexing case, plus an expectSemanticSecondPush wait (an empty result arriving suspiciously fast, under 250ms, earns one more 400ms wait — clients/lsp/index.ts:133-146). For tethys: add a small bounded retry for null goto_definition results during the warmup window (or gate on rust-analyzer's experimental serverStatus). Without this, k543's re-verification pass will silently no-op on early refs and we will read that as "LSP confirmed nothing" — a false negative at scale.

**Volume/scoping answer (this issue's open question).** pi-lens's at-scale nav pattern (clients/module-report-lsp.ts:8-51,194-237): worker pool with concurrency 2, hard symbol cap (theirs 20; ours: cap speculative re-verifications per run, method-call refs first per the 53iv class), every probe clipped to the REMAINING wall-clock budget (their withinRemaining helper) so a slow server degrades the pass instead of hanging it, and a scoped invariant — only query symbols of files you own in this pass; record targets, never recursively re-query them. Note our client is strictly serial one-blocking-round-trip (src/resolve.rs:827-847, single LspClient); a pool needs request pipelining or N clients — decide in design.

**Outcome typing (never conflate absence with failure).** Model each re-verification as a three-way outcome — rebound / confirmed / unavailable (timeout, error, dead server) — mirroring pi-lens's PullDiagnosticsOutcome (found/clean/unavailable, clients/lsp/client.ts:1013-1016) and our CONTEXT.md "query standing" vocabulary. This makes the issue's existing policy ("LSP timeout/error -> leave binding untouched, never demote") type-enforced instead of remembered, and lets the run report "N confirmed, M rebound, K unavailable" instead of a bare count.

**Not needed now (noted so nobody imports it):** pi-lens's per-document version tracking + stale-version nav drop protects against files changing mid-flight in a live agent session. tethys's batch CLI holds files constant during a run — skip until an act-2 long-lived server keeps clients warm across edits (tethys-o4re).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Override policy decided and documented (disagree->rebind+lsp band; confirm->upgrade in place; timeout/error->untouched)
- [ ] Volume scoping decided (method-call-only and/or per-run cap) and documented
- [ ] Pass 3 candidate query covers unresolved UNION speculative
- [ ] Fence: fixture where Pass 2 binds speculatively and rust-analyzer corrects it (requires real rust-analyzer, alongside lsp_resolution.rs)
- [ ] Default (no --lsp) behavior byte-identical to before

title: transport.rs format_uri hardening: test gaps, rename, UNC decision (consolidates 6 issues)
description:
Consolidated hygiene pass over src/lsp/transport.rs (fn format_uri at line 760), bundling six micro-issues filed from PR #64 review rounds: tethys-wzgg, tethys-nsli, tethys-jhv4, tethys-9iwc, tethys-p5rj, tethys-276h (all closed as consolidated-into-this; full details remain readable there via rivets show).

Items:
1. (was tethys-wzgg) Exhaustive-byte invariant test for PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET: iterate 0u8..=127 and assert set membership equals the inverse RFC-3986 must-encode definition, so a typo in the hand-maintained .add(b'X') chain fails loudly instead of silently.
2. (was tethys-nsli) Test that a literal % in a path percent-encodes as %25 — load-bearing for the RFC 3986 round-trip claim; no test currently exercises it.
3. (was tethys-jhv4) Test format_uri returns Ok for a nonexistent path, pinning the I/O-free seam its rustdoc promises (catches a future 'fix' that re-introduces canonicalize/metadata).
4. (was tethys-9iwc) Add format_uri_percent_encodes_unicode_unix to complete the symmetric Windows/Unix test matrix (the Unicode variant is currently #[cfg(windows)]-only).
5. (was tethys-p5rj) Rename format_uri -> absolute_path_to_uri so the 'caller must pass an absolute, canonicalized path' precondition lives in the name, not just prose. Update the one caller in path_to_uri + doc comments.
6. (was tethys-276h, OPTIONAL/deferred) UNC path handling: verify what URI shape rust-analyzer accepts for \\server\share paths and extend if needed. Explicit trigger for doing this: a user report of tethys --lsp failing on a UNC-mounted workspace. May be re-split if the rest of the bundle ships without it.

NOTE: issue bodies of the consolidated issues cite crates/tethys/src/... paths; the repo is now flat (src/lsp/transport.rs).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Exhaustive 0..=127 byte test for PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET
- [ ] Literal-% encodes as %25 test
- [ ] format_uri Ok-for-nonexistent-path test (I/O-free seam)
- [ ] format_uri_percent_encodes_unicode_unix added (symmetric matrix)
- [ ] format_uri renamed to absolute_path_to_uri, caller + docs updated
- [ ] UNC: handled, or explicitly re-deferred with rationale
- [ ] cargo nextest run passes; clippy + fmt clean

title: Surprising connections: cross-file edges scored by file-path distance
description:
Find non-obvious cross-file connections — direct edges between symbols in structurally distant files. High-score pairs indicate unexpected coupling worth investigating.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_surprising tool. The clever insight: rank cross-file edges by path_distance(source_file, target_file) * edge_kind_weight to surface coupling that a human reviewer would never spot in casual inspection.

**Scoring:**
- Path distance: number of directory hops between source and target file paths.
- Edge kind weights (suggested initial values): calls=1.0, references=0.8, type_of=0.7, instantiates=0.7, imports=0.3 (low — imports are expected).
- Score = path_distance * weight; return top-N unique source-target pairs.

**Why this depends on rivets-puyl:**
The call_edges table currently has no kind column. Surprising-connection scoring needs to weight different edge kinds differently, so we need rivets-puyl (Add reference kind tracking to call_edges table) to land first. Without it, we can only score on path distance, which is much weaker signal.

**Implementation:**
- Public API: Tethys::find_surprising_connections(limit) -> Vec<SurprisingEntry { source, target, edge_kind, score }>
- CLI: tethys surprising [--limit N] [--json]
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] find_surprising_connections() API method
- [ ] CLI subcommand with --limit and --json
- [ ] Path-distance helper: count distinct directory components between two relative paths
- [ ] Edge-kind weights configurable or at least documented as constants
- [ ] Filters out same-file edges
- [ ] Unit tests on a multi-directory fixture workspace

title: refs table omits scoped-identifier value uses (crate::Foo / Type::assoc_fn as a value)
description:
Split from tethys-ygjx (shipped bare-identifier fn-as-value; this is the scoped-path sibling). A scoped_identifier in value position — a return value 'crate::OldStruct', or 'Type::assoc_fn' passed as a fn pointer — emits no ref: it is neither a call_expression callee nor a type_identifier. First surfaced by the tethys-jdly design fixture (unit-struct constructor in value position; see ygjx Notes and .tethys-jdly/). Affects deprecated-callers recall for deprecated unit structs constructed via qualified paths.

Distinct from ygjx's bare-identifier case: scoped_identifier resolves through the Rust module-path resolver (crate::/self::/super::), not the flat bare-name resolver, and the local-binding suppression guard does not apply (scoped paths are never local bindings). ygjx's prove-it-prototype measured 305 scoped_identifier occurrences in arg/return value position across tethys src/ — its own noise profile, warranting separate measurement.

Suggested approach: emit a Value ref for scoped_identifier in value position (not callee, not type-position), resolve via ModuleResolver, band speculative per ADR-0003, reuse ygjx's Value ref kind.

title: tethys LSP: verify or extend path_to_uri handling for UNC paths
description: rivets-714v fixes path_to_uri's \?\ extended-length prefix bug for drive-letter paths (C:\...). UNC paths (\\server\share\...) follow a different shape and may need explicit handling: canonicalize() returns them as \\?\UNC\server\share\... on Windows, and the RFC 8089 file URI form for UNC is ambiguous between authority-style (file://server/share/...) and path-style (file:////server/share/...).
design:
## Why deferred from rivets-714v

The rivets-714v fix is bounded to drive-letter paths (the reported and reproduced bug case). UNC paths:

1. Are rare in production for the rivets workspace and typical Cargo users
2. Have ambiguous RFC 8089 mapping (authority vs path-style)
3. Would require a separate decision about which form rust-analyzer accepts
4. Need their own probe + oracle to verify behavior

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a probe that constructs a fixture on a UNC mount (or simulates one via Path manipulation)
- [ ] Verify what URI shape rust-analyzer accepts for UNC paths
- [ ] Extend path_to_uri to produce that shape correctly
- [ ] Add a regression test covering the UNC case

## Trigger condition for promoting priority

If a user reports tethys --lsp failing on a UNC-mounted workspace OR if the test_topology suite ever exercises a UNC fixture and surfaces the gap. Otherwise P4 is appropriate.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] UNC path probe + oracle added (mirrors .rivets-714v/probe.py shape)
- [ ] path_to_uri's behavior on UNC paths verified against rust-analyzer's expectations
- [ ] Regression test for UNC shape added
- [ ] Documented in path_to_uri's rustdoc what URI form is produced for UNC inputs
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-w3z7 (transport.rs format_uri hardening bundle); full details preserved here.

title: C# using static: verify parser emits trailing-dot suffix, add e2e fence for empty-type_name guard
description: Deferred from PR #3 (usgf) review (pr-test-analyzer). The empty-type_name refusal in search_type_members_by_name (symbols.rs) is unit-tested by calling it with type_name empty directly, but no e2e test proves the real pipeline ever produces that empty suffix. For 'using static My.Models.;' the guard is only reachable e2e if the C# extractor stores source_module ending in a dot so rsplit_once('.') yields an empty suffix. ACTION: (1) verify csharp.rs to_import_statement behavior on a trailing-dot directive — keeps the dot, strips it, or drops the import; (2) if kept, add an e2e fence (using static My.Models.; + bare call to a name that exists as a member somewhere in My.Models, assert it does NOT over-match -> None); (3) if stripped/dropped, downgrade the symbols.rs guard comment from 'can reach here' to 'defensive-only' so the rationale matches reality. Low priority: the guard is correct either way; this only aligns test coverage and comment with the parser contract.

title: Test topology mapping
notes: Most primitives exist. Main work is extracting #[test] attributes during parsing and adding a simple query.

title: C# alias using resolution (using Alias = Namespace.Type)
description: Carved out of tethys-usgf (the usgf loop scoped to `using static` only). `using W = My.Models.Widget;` should let a bare `W` reference resolve to the single aliased type. The alias is already stored in Import.alias (probe: stored as `*|My.Models.Widget|W`); mechanism is the explicit-import arm mapping the alias name to one qualified target, not the glob arm. Lowest disambiguation value of the three forms (aliases are author-chosen, usually already unique), so deprioritized. Stays declining until implemented.

title: tethys: plumb CancellationToken through index_with_options
description:
Surfaced by silent-failure-hunter during PR #65 multi-agent review. Pre-existing gap exposed by the rivets-lcb6 fix, not a regression introduced by it.

index_with_options has NO cancellation plumbing today. Before rivets-lcb6, a user Ctrl-C mid-index left the DB with stale-but-valid file_deps from the prior run. After rivets-lcb6, the same Ctrl-C between line 140 (clear_all_file_deps) and the repopulate phases leaves file_deps EMPTY. From the user's perspective, cancellation is now strictly worse: downstream queries (get_file_dependencies, impact analysis) return [] until the next successful index.

Two issues are linked but distinct:
1. (this issue) Plumb a CancellationToken (or std::sync::atomic::AtomicBool / tokio::sync::CancellationToken) through index_with_options. Check it at well-defined yield points: after workspace discovery, after each batch_writer.finish(), before each clear_all_X.
2. (rivets-ml05) Wrap the clear+repopulate in a transaction so abort restores prior state.

Either approach mitigates the worst-case 'empty DB after Ctrl-C' UX. Doing both gives belt + suspenders: transaction handles panics and SQLite errors; cancellation handles user-initiated abort cleanly.

Suggested API surface:
- index_with_options(&self, options: IndexOptions) → unchanged caller-facing default; internally checks an Arc<AtomicBool>
- index_with_options_cancellable(&self, options: IndexOptions, cancel: &CancellationToken) → new variant for CLI / MCP callers

Source: PR #65 multi-agent review aggregate (silent-failure-hunter Suggestion #4).
acceptance_criteria:
* CancellationToken (or equivalent) accepted by indexing entry points
* Cancellation checks at: post-discovery, between batches, before each clear_all_X
* On cancel: structured warn! log noting which phase aborted
* Test: spawn an index, cancel mid-flight, assert clean error return (not panic) and DB state is consistent with rivets-ml05 (either fully prior-state or fully cleared)

title: Pass 2: replace per-ref SELECT cascade with in-memory name->symbols multimap
description: idxperf loop layer (c), deferred: after memoization+batched updates land, the remaining Pass 2 cost is per-ref SELECTs against symbols/files. The symbols table is small enough to hold as a name->[symbol] multimap built once per resolve run, eliminating most round-trips. Deferred from the idxperf design (.idxperf/design.md, approved 2026-06-09) to keep that change behavior-preserving and small.

title: Implement content hashing for file change detection
description: The content_hash field exists in the schema and types but is always passed as None. Implement actual content hashing to enable content-based change detection alongside mtime. This would allow skipping re-indexing when mtime changes but content has not. Three TODO sites: lib.rs (2) and batch_writer.rs (1).
notes: Closed: Duplicate of rivets-3l14 (older, describes the same content_hash TODOs in lib.rs and batch_writer.rs).

title: tethys: fuzzy fallback for --package suggestions (edit distance)
description:
tethys coupling --package <name> uses substring match (case-insensitive) for the "Did you mean:" suggestions when the name isn't found. A user who types rivets_mcp (underscore) for rivets-mcp (hyphen), or rivetsmcp (no separator), gets no suggestion. cargo's own suggestion logic uses Levenshtein-distance fallback in this case.

Reviewer flagged this as "known limitation, could be quality-of-life follow-up" across multiple PR 60 review rounds.

Scope: extend collect_suggestions in crates/tethys/src/cli/coupling.rs to fall back to edit-distance ranking when substring match yields nothing. Use the strsim crate (already in the rust ecosystem) or a hand-rolled DP impl.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Substring match still used first (preserves current behavior for partial matches)
- [ ] Edit-distance fallback when substring yields zero results
- [ ] Configurable distance threshold (default ~3)
- [ ] Unit tests covering: snake-vs-kebab mismatch, missing separator, single-char typo, very distant name yields no suggestion

title: Import-usage detection: a method call makes a same-named unused free-fn import look used (phantom file_dep)
description:
compute_dependencies (src/indexing.rs:896) decides an import is 'used' if the imported name appears in the file's referenced names (built from parsed ExtractedReference.name + path prefixes). It does not distinguish a free-function reference from a method call of the same name. So an UNUSED free-fn import collides with a same-named method and produces a phantom file dependency.

Repro:
  src/a.rs: pub fn process() {}
  src/b.rs:
    use crate::a::process;        // never called as a free fn -> unused
    pub struct T; impl T { pub fn process(&self) {} }
    pub fn run(t: &T) { t.process(); }   // method call, name 'process'
  After index: file_deps contains b.rs -> a.rs (PHANTOM; b does not use a::process).

Independent of tethys-6rlu (the refs_named view does not touch dependency computation, which runs in Pass 1 from parsed names before resolution). Same name-collision class as tethys-53iv but in the import-usage/file_deps layer rather than ref resolution. K-hybrid corroboration (file_deps) may already mitigate some cases; this fixture shows a residual.

Impact: over-reported file dependencies / coupling edges; false cycles possible.
acceptance_criteria:
* In the repro, no b.rs -> a.rs file_dep is produced (the import is correctly unused).
* A method call does not count as a use of a same-named free-function import.
* Legitimate import-usage detection (free-fn import actually called) unchanged (regression).

title: refs table omits macro-token-tree identifiers (split from ygjx as out-of-scope)
description:
Split from tethys-ygjx (which shipped category 1, fn-as-value, and documented this — category 2 — as out-of-scope per its AC). The Rust reference extractor returns early on MACRO_INVOCATION (src/languages/rust.rs:191-201) after recording only the macro NAME; it never descends into the macro's token_tree. Identifiers (including call-shape `foo(...)`) that appear ONLY inside a macro invocation's token tree therefore emit no ref.

Why deferred (measured by ygjx prove-it-prototype, .tethys-ygjx/findings.md): over tethys's own src/ (92 files), token_tree identifiers number 7078 raw; 893 match an in-crate fn name; even restricting to call-shape (identifier directly followed by a '(' token_tree) leaves 591 candidates — overwhelmingly format!/assert!/vec! argument noise. Emitting these needs scope-aware guards well beyond ygjx's budget, so it was split rather than shipped noisy.

Suggested approach: restrict to call-shape identifiers inside token_tree (ident + sibling '(' token_tree), reuse ygjx's containing-function local-binding suppression guard, and band as speculative (ADR-0003) so precision consumers can exclude. Reassess the 591 number after the guard.

Blocks: tethys-y3bx (untested-code) was parked specifically on this macro-token gap. y3bx's blocker should move from ygjx to THIS issue once ygjx merges.
notes: Closed: Shipped: PR #26 merged to main (e809639). New macro_call ref kind for bare call-shaped identifier tokens inside macro token trees: emission with ygjx scope-guard reuse (fences macro_token_call_shapes_emit_exactly_bare_calls + nested/module-level/comment-sibling unit fences), resolution through existing arms (F1 same_file, cross-file unique_workspace fence, F1b collision), unresolved rows dropped (F3), EXCLUDED from call_edges per approved posture (F7; deviation from the issue's band-as-speculative suggestion — kind-exclusion is stronger), deprecated-callers consumes macro sites (F11), macro_rules hygiene (F6), determinism (F9), batch/streaming parity + macro-only file_dep corroboration (F10). Probe==binary 193/193 exact; 11/11 item oracle; analyses byte-identical with/without rows; perf no regression (manual fence, CI trip-wire=tethys-ng1v). The 591-noise fear resolved to 177/clean (shape conflation). Fixed in-branch: gemini comment-sibling finding (verified, fenced). Filed: tethys-7dqj (nested macro names), tethys-ewa7 (path-shaped macro calls). UNBLOCKS tethys-y3bx.

title: ModulePath newtype, ModuleContext::for_file constructor, sealed ModuleResolver trait
description: Type-design hardening for the ModuleResolver seam, deferred from PR #1 review: (1) a ModulePath newtype for canonical '::' qualified names so the canonical-vs-import-format distinction is type-enforced rather than doc-enforced; (2) ModuleContext::for_file(resolver, file, crates, workspace_root) constructor so anchor computation cannot be forgotten or done twice; (3) seal the trait while all impls are in-crate. Reviewer type ratings at time of filing: encapsulation 5/10, invariant expression 4/10, usefulness 8/10, enforcement 5/10. Absorbed from PR #3 (usgf) review (type-design-analyzer): (4) StaticMemberImport.type_name has no non-empty invariant — a trailing-dot 'using static My.Models.;' makes static_member_import emit an empty type_name, caught only by a remote runtime guard in search_type_members_by_name; give static_member_import a smart constructor returning Option so the empty-suffix case becomes None at recognition (keep the downstream guard as defense-in-depth). (5) GlobResolution has inverted None polarity across two structurally identical fields: kinds:None means all-kinds (widening) vs member_kinds:None means static-member-arm-disabled (off); a future language author setting member_kinds:None intending all-member-kinds compiles and silently disables the arm — model arm presence explicitly, e.g. enum MemberArm { Disabled, Enabled(kinds) }, matched at resolve.rs:185.

title: Surface architecture phase stats in tethys index output
description:
The architecture phase populates IndexStats.architecture with packages_recorded/files_assigned/package_deps_recorded counts, but crates/tethys/src/cli/index.rs never displays them. Users running 'tethys index' see 'Indexed 42 files' but no indication that the architecture phase ran or how many packages were detected.

**Fix:** Add a section to the index command output that conditionally prints architecture counts when stats.architecture is Some. Mirror the existing 'Files', 'Symbols', 'References' section style.

Found during final review of rivets-byie.

title: Visibility-tightening analysis: public items that could be crate-private (Rust)
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Act 1 roadmap, first stage (paired with deprecated-callers).

## What to build

An analysis listing items whose declared visibility exceeds their observed use: pub items referenced only from within their own crate are candidates for pub(crate). End-to-end: facade query over symbols + resolved refs, CLI subcommand with table and JSON output, findings carry the Definite/Maybe confidence tiers required by the PRD error posture (suppressions, not accusations).

Tiering decisions that bind here:
- A library crate's root public API may have consumers outside the indexed workspace, which tethys cannot see. Public items of published library crates therefore downgrade to Maybe by default; Definite is reserved for visibility surfaces the workspace fully encloses.
- Re-exports widen the effective visibility surface: an item that is pub-use re-exported must not be flagged just because its direct references are crate-local. This is why the re-export slice blocks this one.
- The fn-as-value refs gap (tethys-ygjx) can make a cross-crate callback-only use invisible. Document as a known limitation; conservative tiering absorbs it.

Self-index sanity check: run against tethys itself and review the findings once by hand — rustc has no lint for this, so there is no automatic oracle; the review documents the observed false-positive rate.

## Blocked by

- tethys-v1w8 (re-export references) — re-exported items would otherwise be false tightening candidates.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: a pub fn used only within its own crate is flagged; a pub fn used cross-crate in the workspace is not
- [ ] Fixture: a pub-use re-exported item is not flagged even when its direct refs are crate-local
- [ ] Published-library-root public items downgrade to Maybe tier by default
- [ ] CLI subcommand with table and JSON output; tier visible in both
- [ ] Known-limitations documented (fn-as-value gap, pointer to tethys-ygjx)
- [ ] One-time reviewed self-index run recorded in the PR (observed false-positive posture)
- [ ] Tests build their own index from fixture workspaces, never an ambient DB
notes: Closed: Shipped: PR #11 merged to main (96021e7). All 6 ACs fenced (see PR body for the AC→fence map); gilfoyle loop artifacts in .tethys-xoxq/ (probes, 15-claim design, 11-slice plan, C14 definite-audit 6/6 grep-confirmed, C15 self-index review 0/26 false Definite). Fixed in-branch: tethys-lwsc (single-segment glob source_module). Filed: tethys-w1e9 (member/module-level), tethys-41lq (C# parity).

title: tethys: replace Mutex<Connection> with a pool; collapse get_package_coupling round-trips
description:
get_package_coupling currently does 3 sequential DB round-trips:
  1. SELECT target package row from arch_coupling JOIN arch_packages
  2. SELECT outgoing neighbors via fetch_neighbors(OUTGOING_SQL)
  3. SELECT incoming neighbors via fetch_neighbors(INCOMING_SQL)

The reason 2 and 3 can't share the connection guard with 1 is the Mutex<Connection> in db/mod.rs is non-reentrant — fetch_neighbors re-acquires the lock internally, so the outer guard from step 1 must drop first. This was the mutex bug caught and documented during PR 60 development.

Reviewer flagged in PR 60 round 5: "If the Mutex<Connection> is ever replaced with a connection pool (e.g. r2d2 or a custom pool), this could be collapsed into fewer round-trips."

This is architecturally the right time to address it — the connection-pool migration would benefit other code paths too (concurrent queries from MCP tools, parallel architecture phase passes, etc.).

Scope:
1. Add a connection pool (r2d2-sqlite or similar) wrapping rusqlite::Connection
2. Either re-architect get_package_coupling to hold one connection across all three queries (single transaction, isolation guarantees) OR fold all three queries into one with UNION + JOIN
3. Remove the documented scope-block workaround in get_package_coupling
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] db/mod.rs uses a connection pool instead of Mutex<Connection>
- [ ] get_package_coupling no longer needs the explicit-drop scope block
- [ ] Benchmark before/after for get_package_coupling on a workspace of >50 crates
- [ ] All existing tests still pass; the mutex non-reentrancy test stays green
- [ ] Document the pool sizing rationale in db/mod.rs

title: Untested-code analysis: symbols unreachable from any test root
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Act 1 roadmap, second stage. Deliberately precedes dead code: same reverse-traversal machinery, cheaper failure mode (a wrong untested claim wastes a test run; a wrong dead claim deletes live code).

## What to build

An analysis reporting symbols that no test can reach: take is_test symbols as roots (test detection already exists), traverse the reference graph through callees, and report indexed symbols outside the covered set. Reuses the existing reachability machinery; CLI subcommand with table and JSON output.

Two decisions from the PRD bind here:
- Traversal must consume the refs table, not only call_edges — call-edge population skips references that occur outside any enclosing symbol, and the analysis must not inherit that blind spot.
- Known limitation to document rather than solve here: functions used only as values (callbacks) have no inbound refs until tethys-ygjx lands, so callback-tested code can appear untested. Related, not blocking — the failure mode is acceptable for this stage.

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately (related: tethys-ygjx).

## Unblocked by tethys-8ym0 (2026-07-15, PR #26)

Macro-token call refs now exist (kind 'macro_call'), so assert-tested code
carries test-inbound edges — the gap this issue was parked on is closed.
BINDING CONSUMPTION NOTE: macro_call is EXCLUDED from call_edges (approved
posture), so the BFS here MUST traverse refs (or a view over refs), NOT
call_edges — refs is now a strict superset and this finally gives AC #2
('a fixture pins a case call_edges alone would miss') a real pinning case:
reuse the y3bx-blocker fixture from tests/macro_token_refs.rs F1. Measured
payoff on self-index: untested 260 -> 235 (.tethys-8ym0/audit.md). The
'Blocked by' section above is stale: ygjx, s8hv, and 8ym0 are all closed.
Residual known limitations to document: method-shape calls in macros
(tethys-9l27), proptest-defined fns (tethys-0nar).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: a function called (transitively) from a #[test] function is excluded from the report; a function no test reaches is included
- [ ] Traversal consumes refs including top-level references — a fixture pins a case that call_edges alone would miss
- [ ] CLI subcommand with table and JSON output
- [ ] Known-limitations section documents the fn-as-value gap with a pointer to tethys-ygjx
- [ ] Works for both Rust and C# test roots (is_test covers both)
- [ ] Tests build their own index from fixture workspaces, never an ambient DB
notes: Closed: Shipped: PR #28 merged to main (8af621d). tethys untested-code [--json]: multi-root forward closure from is_test roots over REFS (call_edges would miss every assert-only-tested fn — 31-symbol gap on self-index, fenced by assert_only_tested_fn_is_covered_and_call_edges_diverge per approved AC#2 rewording). ACs: core rule + S10 pair + S18 collision + kind/is_test scope (report_is_product_fns_outside_test_closure), transitivity/cycles (closure_is_transitive_through_cycles), zero-roots indeterminate posture (zero_roots_is_indeterminate_not_a_dump; CONTEXT.md glossary extended), C# [Fact] parity (csharp_fact_roots_cover_their_callees), binary-seam JSON envelope (cli_json_envelope_through_binary_seam), known-limitations docs pointing at tethys-9l27/0nar/j2r1. Probe==binary exact at two checkpoints (917/706/241); 4/4 item oracle; 6-7ms self-index. Fixed in-branch: none needed. Filed: none (all residual FP classes pre-tracked). Unblocks dead-code stage pending tethys-j2r1.

title: tethys: resolve_super_path uses filesystem-walk semantics, diverges from Rust super:: scoping
design:
Two candidate fixes:

(a) Build a module-tree map at index time: for each `<basename>.rs` adjacent to a `<basename>/` directory, record that the .rs file is the parent-module file of the subdirectory's contents. Then `resolve_super_path` walks the module tree instead of the filesystem.

(b) Special-case the parent-module pattern in resolve_super_path: when `current_file.parent()` contains a sibling `<dir_basename>.rs` (e.g. for `src/parent/child.rs`, check whether `src/parent.rs` exists), treat that .rs file as the parent module and resolve `super::X` relative to *its* directory (which is `src/parent/`, not `src/`).

Option (b) is narrower and matches the common-case Rust idiom (parent.rs + parent/ pattern). Option (a) handles edge cases like `parent/mod.rs` + sibling files. Either way, `resolve_self_path` may need parallel review since it's the inverse semantics question.

Risk: any test or workspace currently relying on the filesystem-walk semantics breaks. The rivets workspace itself doesn't seem to use this shape (otherwise PR #74's rivets-workspace re-index would have surfaced differently).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] resolve_super_path returns `src/parent/X.rs` for ref `super::X` from `src/parent/child.rs` when `src/parent.rs` exists
- [ ] Test in pass2_qualified_paths.rs (or new test file) pins the Rust-spec semantics
- [ ] Existing rivets workspace re-index post-fix: no phantom-rate regression (claim 7 of rivets-3d0s fence holds)
- [ ] Update self_and_super_paths_resolve_via_as_written's docstring to remove the divergence-documentation language (or replace with a comment that says "this is what Rust does, this is what we do, and they now agree")
- [ ] resolve_self_path reviewed for parallel issues; either confirmed correct or follow-up issue filed

title: Add --lsp flag to CLI commands
description:
Add `--lsp` flag to: index, callers, impact

Behavior:
- If --lsp and LSP available: use LSP
- If --lsp and LSP missing: error and halt with helpful message
- No flag: tree-sitter only

Error message should include installation instructions for rust-analyzer.

title: C# using-form coverage: using static, alias usings, global usings
description: Deferred from the jwf9 loop boundary (see .csharp-ns/spec.md decision #5). Plain `using Namespace;` directives resolve through CSharpModuleResolver; three forms still decline: (1) `using static Type;` — bare member (method/const) resolution; (2) `using Alias = Namespace.Type;` — alias-form resolution (Import.alias storage exists; extractor support to be verified); (3) C# 10 `global using` — cross-file propagation of usings, which the per-file resolution model cannot express today. Each form should get its own probe before design; (1) and (2) may surface parser gaps adjacent to tethys-itez/tethys-z45p.
notes: Closed by the usgf loop (branch usgf), SCOPED to `using static` of METHOD members only: CSharpModuleResolver recognizes static usings by type-detection (no schema change) and a union glob arm disambiguates colliding static-method calls. Alias usings (tethys-alus), global usings (tethys-glus), and non-method members — const/static-field/enum, not indexed (tethys-cfme) — were carved out as separate issues. Audit trail in .usgf/.

title: unused-imports: crate-root 'use crate::Foo;' downgraded to MaybeTrait (bare crate resolves to src/ dir)
description:
`tethys unused-imports` reports a genuinely-unused crate-root import such as
