`use crate::Foo;` (where `Foo` is a non-trait workspace symbol defined at the
crate root, e.g. `pub struct Foo;` in `src/lib.rs`) as `MaybeTrait` — hidden by
default, shown only under `--all` — even though the index can prove `Foo` is a
struct and it should be `Definite`.

Reproduced via probe during review:
- `src/lib.rs`: `pub struct Foo;`
- `src/inner.rs`: `use crate::Foo;` (Foo never used)
- Result: finding `name="Foo", source_module="crate", confidence=MaybeTrait`.

Root cause is a resolver asymmetry, not a feature bug. `classify_confidence`
resolves `import.path == ["crate"]` via `resolve_import_segments` ->
`resolve_module_path(["crate"], …)` -> `resolve_crate_path(&[], crate_root)`
(src/resolver.rs:88-91), whose empty-path branch returns the crate-root
**directory**. `relative_path` -> "src"; `get_file_id("src")` -> None
(directories have no rows), so the symbol lookup is skipped and confidence falls
through to `MaybeTrait`.

The sibling workspace-crate arm handles the analogous bare-crate reference
correctly, returning `target.entry_point_file().filter(|p| p.exists())`
(src/resolver.rs:52-58). The `"crate" =>` self-arm does not — that is the gap.

Direction: conservative. The import is still correctly identified as unused;
only its confidence is downgraded (and thus hidden by default). It never
false-accuses a used import.

Source: /code-review max multi-agent review of the unused-imports PR
(PR #4, branch `index-correctness`) — finding #1.
design:
Preferred fix (at the resolver, benefits every consumer of a bare `crate`
module reference): make the empty-tail `crate` case return the crate's
entry-point file when it exists, mirroring the `head =>` workspace-crate arm.
i.e. a bare `crate` module reference resolves to `lib.rs`/`main.rs`, not the
`src/` directory. This likely needs the crate's `entry_point_file()` at the
resolution site, so `resolve_crate_path` (or its caller) may need the crate,
not just `crate_root`.

CAUTION — scope carefully (this is why it is its own PR): the `"crate"` arm and
`resolve_crate_path([])` are used widely (self/super sibling resolution,
`qualified_splits` prefix enumeration, `compute_dependencies`). Changing the
empty-path return value must be checked against all of them. The safest scoping
may be to only return the entry-point file on the import-module resolution path
(`resolve_import_segments`), leaving prefix enumeration untouched.

Narrower alternative (lower blast radius, but fixes only this symptom): handle
it in `classify_confidence` — when `import.path == ["crate"]`, look up the
imported name in the crate entry-point file directly instead of relying on the
resolver's directory result. Does not help any other consumer.

Related: interacts with the nested-group flattening issue (sibling) — both feed
a collapsed `["crate"]` path into the same resolver hole.
acceptance_criteria:
* `use crate::Foo;` for an unused crate-root `pub struct Foo` is reported as
  Definite (not MaybeTrait), with source_module "crate".
* A resolver-level unit test covers bare `crate` module resolution ->
  entry-point file (lib.rs/main.rs), not the src/ directory.
* No regression in cross-file resolution or compute_dependencies: full test
  suite green, and any behavior change to the shared `"crate"` arm is enumerated
  against its other callers (self/super, qualified_splits).
notes: Closed: Fixed in PR #24 (merge b9699c7) through the same resolver seam per the approved tethys-3i35 design: use crate::X now classifies Definite (tests/unused_imports_crate_root.rs); self-index oracle post-fix reports Definite: 0.

title: Rust symbols: trait-impl methods record the TRAIT as parent/qualified prefix, not the implementing type
description: find_impl_type (src/languages/rust.rs:1822) returns the FIRST type_identifier child of an impl_item — for 'impl Trait for Type' that is the TRAIT, so methods get parent_name=Trait and qualified_name='Trait::method' ('ModuleResolver::file_anchor' instead of 'RustModuleResolver::file_anchor'). The REFERENCE side (impl_type_base_name, tethys-53iv receiver typing) correctly uses the 'type' FIELD, so the two sides disagree for every trait impl: receiver-typed method refs carrying path=[ImplementingType] can never hit qualified_exact against the trait-prefixed symbol row (probable silent resolution misses), and name-queries like 'tethys callers RustModuleResolver::file_anchor' miss. 28 trait impls in tethys's own workspace. Fix: find_impl_type should read child_by_field_name('type') (works for inherent impls too — grammar puts the implementing type in the 'type' field, the trait in 'trait'). Discovered by the tethys-aay4 prove-it-prototype (probe used the type field, oracle used stored qualified_names; they disagreed on exactly the trait-impl methods — .tethys-aay4/findings.md). Blocks clean parent_symbol_id semantics: aay4 must not inherit trait-as-parent.
notes: Closed: Fixed in PR #29 (ccb6cf3, absorbed into tethys-aay4 per approved D-A): find_impl_type now delegates to impl_type_base_name — one shared impl-identity rule for symbol and reference sides. Trait-impl methods qualify as Type::method; receiver-typed calls to them now resolve qualified_exact (fence receiver_typed_call_to_trait_impl_method_resolves_qualified_exact was structurally unpassable pre-fix). untested-code gained 2 findings of accuracy (FileId::from/SymbolId::from were false positives).

title: Expand language support: Python, TypeScript, Go
description:
Add language extractors for the three highest-leverage languages beyond Rust and C#. KiroGraph supports 17 languages; tethys can borrow the same tree-sitter grammars.

**Why these three first:**
- Python: huge ecosystem; tree-sitter-python is mature; many Rust crates wrap Python.
- TypeScript: covers frontend; necessary for full-stack repos and any project using Node tooling. Grammar is tree-sitter-typescript (also covers .tsx via tree-sitter-tsx).
- Go: well-defined module system; grammar is small and stable; popular in infrastructure code.

**Approach:**
Tethys's languages/ module is already structured around per-language extractors (common.rs, csharp.rs, rust.rs, tree_sitter_utils.rs). Each new language gets its own file plus grammar dependency in Cargo.toml.

Each extractor needs to handle:
- Symbol extraction (functions, classes/structs, methods, types)
- Visibility (Python lacks formal modifiers; convention is leading underscore)
- Imports / module resolution (Python: import / from; TS: import { } from; Go: import "path")
- Test detection (Python: pytest test_*, unittest.TestCase; TS: describe/it; Go: func TestXxx)
- Reference extraction (call expressions, type annotations)

**Dependencies to add:**
- tree-sitter-python = ''0.23''
- tree-sitter-typescript = ''0.23''
- tree-sitter-go = ''0.23''

**Suggested split:**
This issue is an epic — break into one child issue per language so they can ship incrementally:
- Python language support
- TypeScript language support
- Go language support

Each child should add: grammar dep, extractor module, common test fixture under crates/tethys/tests/fixtures/<lang>/, and integration tests. The tree-sitter parsing infrastructure is already done — adding a language is mostly a query-pattern translation exercise.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Three child issues created (Python, TypeScript, Go), each independently shippable
- [ ] Each language reaches feature parity with the Rust extractor: symbols, refs, imports, test detection
- [ ] Integration test on a representative open-source repo per language
- [ ] tethys index detects language correctly by file extension
- [ ] README updated with new language list

Store unresolved references with symbol_id = NULL

Currently we skip references we can't resolve. Instead, store them with `symbol_id = NULL` and enough context to resolve later.

title: Integrate LSP into callers --lsp
description:
When `--lsp` flag is set for callers query:

1. Return stored references (from DB)
2. Additionally, ask LSP find_references for the symbol
3. Merge results, deduplicate

This catches callers that tree-sitter couldn't resolve during indexing.

title: tethys: add exhaustive-byte invariant test for PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET
description:
PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET in crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs is a hand-maintained chain of 27 .add(b'X') calls. A typo (duplicate, missing entry) is silent — the existing format_uri_* tests only spot-check specific inputs (spaces, %20, Unicode).

Add a test that iterates 0u8..=127 and asserts: for every byte b, set.contains(b) == is_must_encode(b), where is_must_encode is the inverse RFC-3986 definition:
  !b.is_ascii_alphanumeric() && !matches!(b, b'-'|b'.'|b'_'|b'~'|b'/'|b':') || b.is_ascii_control()

~10 lines, runs in microseconds, catches every list-drift bug at CI time.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (type-design-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Test added to crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs#tests module
- [ ] Iterates 0..=127 and asserts set membership matches inverse definition
- [ ] cargo nextest run -p tethys passes
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-w3z7 (transport.rs format_uri hardening bundle); full details preserved here.

title: Rust: nested macro-name refs inside macro token trees are not emitted
description: Inside a macro invocation's token tree, a NESTED macro use is raw tokens (ident + '!' + token_tree) — e.g. the inner matches in assert!(matches!(g(x), Ok(_))). extract_macro_reference records only the OUTER macro name; the inner macro name emits no Macro-kind ref. Impact today is tier-quality only: unused-imports of a macro consumed solely inside another macro is suppressed by the textual word-boundary guard rather than a real ref; hotspots (tethys-7p54) undercount macro usage. Cheap add once tethys-8ym0's token walk exists: ident followed by '!' inside a token tree emits a Macro-kind ref (same unresolved-drop posture as macro_call). Discovered during 8ym0 falsifiable-design shape enumeration (S6); fence macro_token_refs F5 pins current non-emission as a TRIPWIRE.

title: Implement deferred dependency resolution for circular dependencies
description:
Currently, dependency detection fails silently when the target file hasn't been indexed yet. This happens with circular dependencies (A→B, B→A) where the first-indexed file's dependency on the second file is lost.

**Current behavior**: Dependencies can only be recorded to files already in the database. Files indexed earlier may not have their dependencies to later-indexed files recorded.

**Proposed solution**: Instead of two full passes, queue unresolved dependencies and retry after the initial pass:

1. First pass: Index all files, queue dependencies that fail to resolve (target not in DB)
2. Resolution passes: Retry pending queue until it stops shrinking
3. Log any remaining unresolved (external crates or truly missing files)

**Convergence check**: `pending.len() < prev_count` ensures we don't loop forever.

**Benefits**:
- More efficient than re-indexing everything
- Handles circular dependencies elegantly
- Minimal code change to existing `index()` flow
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `PendingDependency` struct created
- [ ] `index_file_atomic` modified to return pending dependencies instead of silently dropping
- [ ] `resolve_pending()` method implemented
- [ ] Convergence loop in `index()` with shrinking check
- [ ] Test: circular dependency (A→B, B→A) both directions detected
- [ ] Test: convergence terminates for external crates
- [ ] Debug logging for unresolved dependencies after all passes

title: Store imports during indexing (Rust + C#)
description:
During file indexing, extract import statements and store them in the imports table.

For Rust: parse `use` statements via existing `extract_use_statements()`
For C#: parse `using` directives via existing `extract_using_directives()`

Map both to the common imports table format.

title: Dead-code finder: symbols with zero incoming references
description:
Find unexported / non-public symbols with no incoming references — candidates for removal. Trivial to implement on the existing schema; high developer-value signal.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_dead_code tool and CLI command.

**Implementation:**
Single SQL query:
  SELECT s.* FROM symbols s
  LEFT JOIN call_edges c ON c.callee_symbol_id = s.id
  LEFT JOIN refs r ON r.symbol_id = s.id
  WHERE c.callee_symbol_id IS NULL
    AND r.symbol_id IS NULL
    AND s.visibility != 'public'
  LIMIT ?;

Filtering to non-public is important — public/exported symbols may be used by consumers outside the indexed workspace, so reporting them as dead would generate false positives.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::find_dead_code(limit: usize) -> Result<Vec<Symbol>>

**CLI:**
- tethys dead-code [--limit N] [--json]

**Caveats to document:**
- Macro-expanded references aren't tracked, so macro-only call sites can produce false positives.
- Trait-method dispatch through dyn Trait may not appear as a direct call edge; investigate before reporting.

## Transferred from tethys-9z7i slice 4 (2026-07-04)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERION: dead-code traversal treats speculative-band edges
(refs_banded view, ADR-0003) as SUPPRESSIONS — a speculative 'maybe
someone calls this' edge suppresses a dead-code finding while staying
excludable from precision consumers (callers already supports
--exclude-speculative, PR #14). The provenance infrastructure (strategy
column, band view, edge filtering) shipped in PRs #12/#13/#14.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] find_dead_code() API method
- [ ] CLI subcommand tethys dead-code with --limit and --json
- [ ] Filters out public/exported symbols
- [ ] Documents known false-positive sources in module docs
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_dead_code (sibling rivets-o4re)
- [ ] Tests on a fixture with both clearly-dead and clearly-live symbols

title: qualified_exact binds first match when duplicate qualified names exist across crates
description: db/symbols.rs get_symbol_by_qualified_name uses query_row with no uniqueness check: two crates each declaring Config::load produce duplicate symbols.qualified_name rows, and the qualified_exact arm (resolve.rs fallback_symbol_search) binds whichever has the lowest rowid — arbitrary, not declined. Pre-existing for scoped calls (User::new()); tethys-53iv widens the qualified_exact population (receiver-derived T::m method refs) so the surface grows. title: Add CSharpLsProvider
C# parity for deprecated-callers: attribute extraction + [Obsolete] detection
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Act 1, C# parity user story: both indexed languages get the same health signals.

## What to build

Extend the deprecated-callers analysis to C#. The blocker today is upstream of the analysis: symbol attribute extraction is Rust-only, so [Obsolete] is never recorded. This slice cuts through that layer end-to-end: extract attributes for C# symbols during parsing (at minimum Obsolete, stored the same way Rust attributes are), detect obsolete-marked symbols, and surface their call sites through the same CLI subcommand with identical table/JSON shape.

C# dependency resolution is deliberately more conservative than Rust (namespace/using corroboration), so caller lists may be narrower — that is acceptable and consistent with the error posture; do not loosen resolution to widen results.

## Blocked by

- tethys-jdly (deprecated-callers analysis, Rust) — this slice extends its command surface.

- [ ] C# fixture: call sites of an [Obsolete] method are listed by the same CLI subcommand
- [ ] C# attribute extraction stores at minimum the Obsolete attribute alongside symbols, matching the Rust storage shape
- [ ] Obsolete message/error flag surfaced when present
- [ ] JSON output shape identical across languages
- [ ] Rust deprecated-callers behavior unchanged (regression)

Beyond the ticket: same-language guard on Path B + ambiguity tiering (design C9) — fixes latent jdly cross-language bleed; variable-receiver C# calls surface as Maybe via unresolved-qualified (Path B), verified 19/19 on real repo data.

title: cfg_attr-wrapped attributes invisible to attribute-based analyses (#[cfg_attr(pred, deprecated)])
description:
Found during tethys-jdly falsifiable-design input-shape enumeration.

`#[cfg_attr(feature = "x", deprecated)]` stores attribute name 'cfg_attr' (per the schema comment in src/db/schema.rs: name = leading path identifier), so deprecated-callers and any future attribute-keyed analysis miss conditionally-applied attributes.

Fix sketch: during extraction, when name == 'cfg_attr', additionally emit a row for the wrapped attribute (second arg onward), possibly with a marker that it is conditional. Niche in practice; jdly documents it as a known limitation.
notes: Closed: Fixed: PR #15. Repro fences: cfg_attr_wrapped_attributes_are_emitted + comma/multi-attr stress + cfg_attr_deprecated_fn_is_listed_with_callers. Nested-recursion refinement filed separately.

title: visibility-tightening channel-(c) fence is vacuous: a_lib::qonly() resolves, qualified-text exclusion never exercised
description:
tests/visibility.rs channel-(c) fence (unresolved_qualified_excludes, ~line 121) claims a_lib::qonly() from b-app 'stays unresolved (the tethys-3i35 decline class)' so that only its qualified reference_name text can exclude qonly from visibility-tightening findings. Empirically false (verified 2026-07-12 during tethys-3i35 slice 1, faithful fixture replica): the ref RESOLVES via qualified_module_fallback (as-written single-segment workspace-crate arm claims a-lib/src/lib.rs, tail qonly found), so the exclusion flows through the resolved-ref channel and the qualified-TEXT channel is never exercised — the assertion passes vacuously. Structurally unrelated to the tethys-3i35 fix (which touches only paths that are exactly ['crate']); pre-existing.

Also stale: comments at tests/visibility.rs:75-79 and :121-122 cite 'the tethys-3i35 decline class' for this workspace-crate-prefixed shape, which 3i35 never governed (3i35 = bare crate:: prefix), and 3i35 is now fixed.

Fix shape: switch the fixture's qonly caller to a genuinely-undecidable path (external prefix, e.g. otherzz::qonly() — external crates are never indexed, so the fence cannot rot as resolution improves; same pattern applied to tests/deprecated_callers.rs Path B fixture in the 3i35 PR), re-pin the expected channel, and update both comments. Also re-check the sibling channel tests in the same file for the same vacuity (their cfg-twin collision shapes probably still decline, but verify empirically).

title: ci: perf trip-wire test — full index of a generated workspace under a generous ceiling
description:
From the 2026-07-12 pi-lens implementation review.

## Motivation

tethys has criterion benches but ZERO asserted perf bounds (verified 2026-07-12: tests/ contains only a trivial duration-greater-than-zero check at tests/concurrency_and_filesystem.rs:192; benches assert nothing; no regression gate). Our own history proves the exposure: the pre-idxperf indexing path was 15.74s -> 0.44s (35x; ~96% fdatasync-bound from per-row autocommits, BENCHMARKS.md:33-36). A trip-wire would have caught that class of regression in PR CI the day it landed.

## Methodology (pi-lens perf-harness pattern, translated to Rust)

pi-lens asserts event-loop occupancy budgets on scaled synthetic fixtures (tests/support/perf-harness.ts; budgets 100-300ms at 1200-5000 generated files) with the stability discipline that makes CI perf assertions trustworthy: budgets set at 3-8x the measured cost of the healthy implementation — wide enough to absorb shared-runner noise (plus retry:2 in their runner), narrow enough that an order-of-magnitude regression trips. Their sampler mechanism does not transfer (event-loop specific); the budget PHILOSOPHY is the whole lesson: huge headroom -> zero flakes -> a red trip-wire is believed.

Rust translation: a plain #[test] that generates a synthetic workspace (reuse benches/common.rs temp-workspace harness), runs a full batch index, asserts wall-clock under a generous ceiling. Example shape (calibrate real numbers at implementation): a 200-file fixture measuring ~1s healthy asserts under 8s.

## Scope

- One or two tests: full index; optionally reindex-unchanged (the fast path staying fast).
- Runs in PR CI (that is the point) — keep the fixture small enough; if it cannot be made fast enough, ignored + nightly is the fallback, but prefer PR CI.
- Explicit NON-goals: fine-grained thresholds or trend detection. Nightly criterion run-and-record (tethys-xpc4) owns trends; this owns cliffs. Rationale mirrors xpc4's no-auto-thresholds decision and tethys-09wx's zero-false-alarm triggers: a noisy gate trains people to ignore red.

Related: tethys-xpc4 (complementary nightly benches), rivets-o3s8 (staleness sweep cost — a candidate second trip-wire once incremental reindex lands via tethys-q8qw).

title: Implement LspProvider trait + RustAnalyzerProvider
tethys: push name/Ca/Ce sort to SQL ORDER BY (keep instability in Rust)
description:
get_coupling_metrics (architecture.rs) fetches all rows from arch_coupling unsorted, then sorts in Rust. This was a deliberate choice for the Instability variant — the formula lives only in CouplingMetrics::instability() and can't be replicated in SQL without duplicating logic. For the other three sort variants (Name, Afferent, Efferent) the choice was made by consistency, not necessity.

Reviewer flagged in PR 60 round 7: "At scale (monorepos with hundreds of crates) the full fetch + in-memory sort could become noticeable... consider adding a follow-up issue for DB-side sorting of the non-instability variants (name, Ca, Ce) if performance ever becomes a concern."

Trigger condition: a workspace with hundreds of crates where get_coupling_metrics becomes a measurable hot path.

Scope:
- Add ORDER BY clauses to the query in get_coupling_metrics for Name/Ca/Ce variants
- Keep the Rust-side sort for Instability (formula stays in one place)
- The current secondary sort (by package name) for ties stays in the SQL ORDER BY

Note: SQLite COLLATE NOCASE may be wanted for the Name variant to match the locale-insensitive lexicographic sort Rust's String::cmp provides today.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] get_coupling_metrics emits ORDER BY for CouplingSort::Name, ::Afferent, ::Efferent
- [ ] Instability sort still happens in Rust (formula stays in one place)
- [ ] Tie-breaking by name remains deterministic across variants
- [ ] Benchmark before/after on a synthetic >200-crate workspace

title: Decide unused-imports / deprecated-callers handling of newly-indexed test code (post tethys-s8hv)
description: tethys-s8hv indexes inline-module (unit-test) code, which unused-imports and deprecated-callers now see (panic-points already filters is_test=0; visibility-tightening is handled inside s8hv). Decide per analysis: should unused-imports report unused imports inside #[cfg(test)] modules? Should deprecated-callers count test call sites (s8hv updates its fence to the new behavior — confirm that's the intended policy)? A shared 'exclude test code' lever (is_test / test-file, or a test-context flag per negative-space #2 of the s8hv design) may be the clean mechanism. Blast radius at s8hv time was 1 test, so this is policy cleanup, not a live break.

title: unused-imports/parser: nested-group 'use crate::{util::Helper}' drops intermediate path segment
description:
A nested-group import such as `use crate::{util::Helper};` (or
`use crate::{db::{self}, util::Helper}`) loses its intermediate path segment.
`tethys unused-imports` then reports the wrong `source_module` — "crate" instead
of "crate::util" — in both `--json` and human output, and downgrades confidence
to `MaybeTrait` even for a workspace struct.

Reproduced via probe during review:
- `src/util.rs`: `pub struct Helper;`
- `src/lib.rs`: `use crate::{util::Helper};` (Helper never used)
- Result: finding `name="Helper", source_module="crate", confidence=MaybeTrait`.

Root cause: `collect_use_list_names` (src/languages/rust.rs) keeps only the
FINAL segment of a nested `scoped_identifier` list member (it calls
`parse_scoped_identifier` and takes just the name), discarding the intermediate
`util`. `parse_scoped_use_list` fills `path` only from the OUTER list's path
child. So `import.path` collapses to `["crate"]` and `imported_names` to
`["Helper"]`.

The deeper cause is the `ImportStatement` model itself: it carries ONE
`path: Vec<String>` for MANY `imported_names: Vec<String>`, so it structurally
cannot represent `use a::{b::C, d::E}` where each name has a distinct path.

Impact beyond display: because `import.path` collapses to `["crate"]`,
downstream module resolution (`classify_confidence`, and in principle
`compute_dependencies`) resolves the wrong module — compounding with the
`resolve_crate_path([])` gap tracked in the sibling issue.

Direction: conservative for unused-imports (hides/downgrades), but the wrong
`source_module` string is user-visible in the report.

Source: /code-review max multi-agent review of the unused-imports PR
(PR #4, branch `index-correctness`) — finding #2.
design:
This is a model change and should be scoped as a design step first (it ripples
to every import consumer), which is why it is its own PR.

Two viable shapes:

1. Expand nested groups at parse time into multiple `ImportStatement`s, one per
   leaf, each carrying its full path (`use crate::{util::Helper, db::Conn}` ->
   two statements: path `["crate","util"]` name `Helper`, and path
   `["crate","db"]` name `Conn`). Least invasive for consumers — each keeps
   assuming one path per statement.

2. Give the model a per-name path (each imported name resolves against its own
   segment list). More expressive but touches every reader.

Touches: `ImportStatement` (src/languages/common.rs), the Rust use-parser
(`collect_use_list_names`, `parse_scoped_use_list`, `parse_use_declaration`),
and every consumer of `path` + `imported_names` (`compute_dependencies`,
`unused_imports`, the C# `UsingDirective` converter). Prefer option 1 unless a
consumer genuinely needs the grouped form.

Note: fixing this narrows but does not fully close the crate-root case — the
resolver hole in the sibling issue (`resolve_crate_path([])` -> src/ dir) must
also land for `use crate::Foo;` to reach Definite.
acceptance_criteria:
* `use crate::{util::Helper};` (unused) reports source_module "crate::util"
  and, with the index present, confidence Definite (given the sibling
  resolver fix).
* Heterogeneous nested groups (`use a::{b::C, d::E}`) resolve each imported
  name against its own full path.
* Parser tests cover nested-group path retention; full suite green.
* No behavior change for flat imports and single-name statements.
notes: Closed: Fixed: PR #16. Repro fences: nested_group_members_keep_intermediate_segments, mixed_group_plain_members_share_group_path, nested_group_import_reports_full_source_module (source_module crate::util + Definite restored).

title: deprecated-callers: obsolete/deprecated CONSTRUCTORS read Clean while callers exist (construct refs bind the type symbol)
description:
Found during tethys-haw5 falsifiable-design (input-shape enumeration).

When [Obsolete] / #[deprecated] decorates a CONSTRUCTOR, its reference sites are construction expressions (`new T(...)` / `T::new()` struct-literal-adjacent shapes). Construct refs resolve to the TYPE symbol, not the constructor symbol, so the constructor entry in deprecated-callers shows sites=[] and lands in the "Clean (migration done)" bucket — a misleading green verdict while callers still exist.

Affects both languages in principle; C# is the sharper case because [Obsolete] on ctors is idiomatic (steering users to factory methods).

Possible directions (design needed): attach construct refs of the parent type to obsolete ctors when the type has exactly one ctor; or tier such entries Maybe with an explicit "sites bind the type" caveat; or suppress the Clean verdict for ctor symbols with construct refs on their parent type.

title: Stale comment: file_deps_idempotency tests claim an mtime/size short-circuit that does not exist
description: tests/file_deps_idempotency.rs comments describe an mtime/size short-circuit in the indexer, but no such short-circuit exists — every discovered file is re-parsed and re-written each run. Comment-only fix: reword to describe actual behavior (or fold into tethys-q8qw when incremental updates land, which would make a short-circuit real). Discovered by the tethys-dhxo sweep agent (PR #25) while building the orphan-file repro; provenance: sweep of 2026-07-14.

title: Scope SQLite busy_timeout based on read vs write workload
description:
The current Index::open sets a 30-second busy_timeout for all operations. This was chosen to handle concurrent test parallelism (rivets-byie's architecture phase writes interact with tests sharing the rivets workspace DB).

**The concern:** if a user runs an interactive command like 'tethys coupling' while a background 'tethys index' is running, the read-side query could silently block for up to 30s before its first SQL operation succeeds. With WAL mode, true read queries don't block on writes, but the schema/pragma writes in Index::open itself can. The user sees no feedback — just a long pause.

**Possible fix shapes:**
- Set a short busy_timeout (500ms or 1s) by default and override it inside indexing operations only.
- Add an Index::open_read_only or similar that uses a shorter timeout for query-only commands.
- Surface a 'waiting for lock' message after 2-3s if the operation is still blocked.

**Why deferred:** the concurrent-interactive scenario is rare in practice (most users run one tethys command at a time). The 30s timeout is conservative but safe. Worth tuning if anyone reports a real UX issue.

Discovered during second-round PR #60 review.

title: Rust: path-shaped calls inside macro token trees emit no ref
description: tethys-8ym0 ships bare call-shape identifiers inside macro token trees; path-shaped call tokens (ident '::' ident + '('-token_tree, e.g. m::f(...) or Type::assoc(...) inside assert!/proptest!) stay unextracted — assembling a qualified path from raw tokens needs its own segment-walk and resolves through the module-path machinery, not the bare-name arms. Measured by the 8ym0 probe on self-index: 178 raw scoped-call shapes, only 3 matching in-crate fns, all inside proptest! bodies that cannot attach to a containing fn anyway until tethys-0nar (macro-defined fns not indexed). Sibling of tethys-i09d (scoped values in normal AST) and tethys-9l27 (method shapes in macros). Re-measure on a macro-heavier workspace before investing. Fence macro_token_refs F4 pins current non-emission as a TRIPWIRE.

title: Audit tethys tracing log messages for structured-field consistency
description: Project convention (per CLAUDE.md 'Structured Logging with tracing') is structured fields, not string interpolation in the message. PR #63 added some logs that bake the operation name into prose ('compute_dependencies: file not in any known crate; skipping') rather than using a structured field like operation = "compute_dependencies". Codebase has a mix of both styles.
design:
## Concrete examples flagged in PR #63 review

The three new trace/debug sites added in PR #63 use prose-style operation names:

- resolve.rs::resolve_refs_for_file: 'File not in any known crate; skipping Pass-2-imports'
- indexing.rs::compute_dependencies: 'compute_dependencies: file not in any known crate; skipping'
- indexing.rs::compute_dependencies_from_stored: 'compute_dependencies_from_stored: file not in any known crate; skipping'

Project convention would prefer:

```rust
debug!(
    operation = "compute_dependencies",
    file = %current_file.display(),
    "File not in any known crate; skipping"
);
```

## Scope

This is broader than just PR #63 sites. The whole tracing surface in tethys should be audited. CLAUDE.md provides examples of the desired pattern; grep for tracing macros and identify call sites that don't match.

## Why P4

Cosmetic. Doesn't affect correctness. Worth doing during a quiet maintenance window, not as part of a bug fix PR.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Inventory all tracing call sites in crates/tethys/src/
- [ ] For each, classify: matches structured-field convention OR uses string interpolation
- [ ] Update string-interpolated sites to use structured fields per CLAUDE.md
- [ ] Bonus: add a lint or test that flags the pattern (optional)

title: search: FTS5 bm25 ranking with identifier-aware tokenization
description:
From the 2026-07-12 pi-lens implementation review. Serves Act 2 (agent-facing discovery — PRD tethys-l6nt user stories 15/16); sequence accordingly.

## Current state (verified)

src/db/symbols.rs:73-87 — search is one LIKE '%query%' over name + qualified_name. Ranking is 2 keys: exact-name-first (BINARY collation — so a case-variant exact query MISSES the boost while the LIKE itself is ASCII-case-insensitive; inconsistent), then shorter qualified_name. No tokenization: a query "foo bar" matches only the literal substring. Limit hardcoded 100 (src/lib.rs:285-286). No FTS5/bm25/MATCH anywhere in the repo (grepped 2026-07-12). Fast (159 us, BENCHMARKS.md) but relevance-blind.

## Design

SQLite FTS5 gives bm25() natively (default k1=1.2 b=0.75 — the same constants pi-lens hand-rolled). What FTS5 does NOT give, port from pi-lens clients/word-index.ts:

1. Identifier tokenization (word-index.ts:105-133): populate the FTS table with pre-split tokens at index time (external-content or contentless table keyed to symbols rowid; no custom tokenizer needed). Index the WHOLE identifier AND its parts so exact identifiers outrank fragments. Split passes: non-alphanumeric runs (snake/kebab/::), camelCase boundary lower-to-upper, acronym boundary (upper followed by upper+lower — use the lookahead form, ReDoS-linear per their Sonar S5852 note), letter-digit boundaries both directions. Lowercase, min token length 2, no stemming, small language-agnostic stopword list (~48 entries). Examples: getUserByID -> [getuserbyid, get, user, by, id]; HTTPServer -> [httpserver, http, server]; MAX_RETRY_2 -> [max_retry_2, max, retry, 2].
2. Scoring priors (word-index.ts:81-89, 372-386) — adapt, upgraded by our data: test-path penalty x0.3 (we have is_test + partial index idx_symbols_is_test — better grounded than their path regex); import-centrality boost x(1 + ln(1+importers)/4) (we have REAL resolved file_deps; theirs was a snapshot approximation). Column weighting: bm25() per-column weights, name over qualified_name.
3. FTS5 maintenance: same DB, same transaction as symbol writes (insert path in src/db/symbols.rs; delete-by-file on reindex) so the FTS index can never go stale independently of the index — no new query-standing domain.

## Open decisions (settle at design time)

- Multi-token semantics: FTS5 default is implicit AND; pi-lens chose disjunctive OR with score summing. Recommendation: AND with OR fallback on zero hits; decide and document.
- Whether tethys search keeps a substring fallback path for non-identifier queries (paths, operators).

## Pitfalls to avoid (documented pi-lens bugs, word-index agent report 2026-07-12)

- Relevance-blind truncation: their 6000-file cap slices in readdir order — never cap before ranking.
- Output claims drifting from implementation: their startLine was documented "best-scoring hit" but implemented "first occurrence". Keep displayed semantics honest.
- Their tf=distinct-lines damping trick applies to file-granular indexes; indexing one row per SYMBOL makes it moot — do not cargo-cult it.

Related: tethys-o4re (the MCP symbol_search tool consumes this), tethys-7p54 (hotspots can share the log-damped centrality computation).

title: Compute content hash for indexed files
description:
The schema supports `content_hash` but it's always `None`. See `batch_writer.rs:238`, `lib.rs:643`, `lib.rs:803`.

Content hashing would provide more reliable change detection than mtime/size alone, useful for:
- Detecting changes after file touch without content change
- Cache key generation
- Detecting identical content in different files

## 2026-07-12 pi-lens implementation review — role and algorithm

**Role clarified:** the hash is the CONFIRMATION tier of incremental reindex (tethys-q8qw): mtime/size drift nominates candidates (existing src/reindex.rs:202-223), the content hash confirms real change; unchanged hash reuses parsed results with no re-parse. Without q8qw the column is still useful for byte-identical freeze checks, but q8qw is the consumer that makes it load-bearing (dep edge added: q8qw depends on this).

**Current state (verified 2026-07-12):** the column exists (src/db/schema.rs:12) but is always written None (src/indexing.rs:778); src/types.rs:722 documents "algorithm TBD, not yet implemented."

**Algorithm decision to settle here:** pi-lens uses sha256 (clients/review-graph/builder.ts:354-411). For tethys, blake3 is the stronger candidate (much faster, SIMD, well-maintained crate; adversarial collision resistance is not a requirement for a local cache) — but it is a new dependency to vet against deny.toml. sha2 is already common in dependency trees. Decide, document in the schema comment, and note that changing algorithms later just invalidates the confirmation tier gracefully (hash mismatch -> re-parse), so this is not a one-way door.

**Population strategy:** hash at parse time — file content is already in memory in the indexing pipeline (the write site is indexing.rs:778's None today), so cost is one pass over bytes we already hold. On the reindex path, hash only NOMINATED candidates (stat-drifted files), never the whole tree per run.

title: Resolution NULLs refs.reference_name — name-based queries over refs are silently empty for resolved symbols
description:
Pass-2 resolution sets reference_name=NULL once a ref resolves to an in-crate symbol (src/db/references.rs:157: UPDATE refs SET symbol_id=?2, reference_name=NULL WHERE id=?1). Initial storage also only populates reference_name when symbol_id IS NULL (src/indexing.rs:779, src/batch_writer.rs:329).

Net effect: refs.reference_name is populated ONLY for UNRESOLVED references. Every reference that resolves to an in-crate symbol keeps just a numeric symbol_id and has reference_name=NULL.

Consequence (the real bug): name-based SQL over the refs table is silently incomplete. SELECT ... FROM refs WHERE reference_name='foo' returns 0 for any in-crate symbol foo that resolved — even though the ref rows exist. The only correct name query is a join: JOIN symbols s ON r.symbol_id=s.id WHERE s.name='foo'. External/ad-hoc SQL consumers have no way to know this and get vacuous results that look like 'no references'.

This is the actual root cause behind the now-closed tethys-zp2j: a kiro-control-center plan-lint gate filtering refs.reference_name='make_service' found the refs 'vacuous' and fell back to the imports table. The refs were present (resolved, reference_name NULL); the gate measured the wrong column.

Verified (self-index): bare free fn node_text => 0 rows WHERE reference_name='node_text', but 60 refs + 37 call_edges via JOIN symbols on symbol_id (kind='call'). Globally: 1884 resolved call-refs carry reference_name=NULL; 7673 unresolved carry a name.
design:
DECISION (falsifiable-design 2026-06-30): add an additive SQL view 'refs_named' (LEFT JOIN refs->symbols, COALESCE(reference_name, symbols.name) AS name). NOT option 1 (overload reference_name).

Rationale (subtractive sweep): overloading reference_name to always carry the name breaks two consumers that assume 'reference_name populated => unresolved': panic_points.rs:51,103 (WHERE reference_name IN ('unwrap','expect') with no symbol_id guard -> in-crate unwrap/expect gain false panic sites) and indexing.rs:1003 compute_dependencies_from_stored (resolved same-file names leak into refs_set -> phantom file_deps). The view leaves reference_name semantics untouched (no consumer changes) and computes name live (no rename staleness).

Probe+oracle agreed (5 fns, counts 1-59). Cheapest falsifier passed against the real view on a fresh index. Full design + 7-claim falsification table: .tethys-6rlu/design.md. Implementation: add CREATE VIEW IF NOT EXISTS refs_named to src/db/schema.rs; document refs_named as the name-query surface.

NOTE: on-disk index was stale during probing -> every regression fence must build its OWN index from a fixture, never query an ambient DB.
acceptance_criteria:
* After indexing + Pass-2 resolution, a ref to an in-crate symbol foo is retrievable by name from refs WITHOUT manually joining symbols (reference_name stays populated, or a documented view/column provides it).
* A regression fixture pins the node_text-style case: name-query count over refs == symbol_id-join count for a known in-crate free function.
* Storage decision (denormalize vs view) recorded in the issue.
* Existing resolved-ref consumers (get_callers, call_edges, dead-code) unaffected; no double-counting.
notes: Closed: Implemented on feat/tethys-6rlu-refs-named: additive refs_named SQL view (COALESCE(reference_name, symbols.name) over LEFT JOIN), auto-applied on every Index::open. 7 regression fences (C1-C7); full suite green, clippy clean, prove-it oracle 5/5. C1 narrowed to resolved+bare-unresolved (unresolved qualified refs keyed by qualified path — tethys-3i35); C5 folded into the root invariant. reference_name semantics untouched -> no consumer change.

title: Add integration tests for cross-file resolution
description:
Add tests in tests/indexing.rs:

- cross_file_callers_via_explicit_import
- cross_file_callers_via_qualified_path  
- cross_file_callers_via_glob_import
- csharp_cross_file_via_namespace

Each test creates a multi-file workspace and verifies callers() finds cross-file references.

title: qualified_module_fallback tail lookup does not chase re-exports in the claimed file
description:
A qualified call like crate::db::build_qualified_name() stays UNRESOLVED even though it compiles: the qualified_module_fallback split crate::db claims src/db/mod.rs (file exists in index), but the tail lookup search_symbol_by_qualified_name_in_file('build_qualified_name', db/mod.rs) misses because the fn is DEFINED in src/db/files.rs and only re-exported by db/mod.rs (pub(crate) use files::build_qualified_name; at src/db/mod.rs:55). Per the C6 driver contract the claimed-but-tail-missing split is abandoned, and the shorter ['crate'] split cannot help (tail lives in a submodule).

Population measured during the tethys-3i35 probe (2026-07-12, self-index of 96 files / 19,293 refs): ALL 13 unresolved crate::-prefixed refs in tethys's own index are this shape (crate::db::build_qualified_name x4, crate::db::parse_* x3, crate::db::normalize_path, crate::types::SymbolId::from x2, crate::types::FileId::from, crate::types::DatabaseStats::default, crate::types::ArchStats::default — the types::X::from/default ones additionally involve trait-impl method tails). See .tethys-3i35/findings.md.

Distinct from tethys-z9mr (relative IMPORT paths) and tethys-pv7w (re-export targets carrying inbound refs): this is the qualified-CALL tail lookup inside an already-claimed file not chasing use-decls.

Impact: callers/impact/reachability under-count for submodule symbols invoked through their parent-module re-export path; dead-code prerequisite class.

Likely fix shape: when the tail misses in the claimed file's symbols, consult the claimed file's imports rows (which record pub use re-exports) and follow one hop to the defining file; or resolve the tail against the re-export's source_module recursively with a hop bound.

title: Nested C# namespace blocks don't participate in namespace resolution
description: namespace Outer1 { namespace Inner1 { ... } } stores two separate un-dotted Module symbols (Outer1, Inner1) with NO parent_symbol_id chain (probed 2026-06-06, .csharp-ns/probe-findings.md), so the namespace map (keyed on Module symbol names) can never match a `using Outer1.Inner1;` directive. Pre-existing limitation — the deleted resolve_csharp_dependencies post-pass had the same gap. Fix requires the C# extractor to either parent-chain nested namespace modules or store the dotted name; then the map builder reconstructs keys. Modern C# rarely uses nested blocks (dotted declarations and file-scoped namespaces both work today), hence deferred. Surfaced during the jwf9 loop design (.csharp-ns/design.md claim C2).

title: tethys: implement depth-limited transitive impact analysis
description: The --depth flag in impact analysis is accepted but not implemented. Currently, transitive analysis always explores the full dependency graph. Implement depth limiting to control how many levels of indirection to follow.
notes: Closed: Already implemented: --depth was wired through tethys impact in PR #57 (verified at src/cli/impact.rs:16,24 — depth passed to get_symbol_impact/get_impact). Remaining follow-ups tracked in tethys-w0qw (--depth 0 semantics) and tethys-3yxn (callers --transitive parity).

title: refs table omits bare free-function call references (only method & qualified calls recorded)
description:
Tethys does not record references to bare free-function calls (e.g. make_service(), validate_kiro_project_path()) in the refs table. Method calls (.unwrap(), .expect()) and path-qualified calls (PathBuf::from, InstallMode::from) ARE recorded, so the gap is specifically unqualified free-function invocations.

This makes callers, impact, reachability, and any SQL-over-refs analysis silently incomplete for free functions: a free function appears to have zero call-site references even when it is called throughout the codebase.

Discovered while designing a plan-lint gate (kiro-control-center kiro-6g6r) that intended to detect make_service() calls via refs.reference_name. The refs half was vacuous; we fell back to the imports table (which is complete and accurate). The gate works without this, but the underlying indexer gap is real and affects caller/impact correctness.
design:
Repro (tethys 0.1.0, fresh index of crates/kiro-control-center):

  tethys --workspace . index    # 74 files, 1815 symbols, 20235 refs
  DB=.rivets/index/tethys.db

  # Bare free-fn calls -> 0 refs, despite being called in every command wrapper:
  sqlite3 $DB "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM refs WHERE reference_name='make_service';"               # 0
  sqlite3 $DB "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM refs WHERE reference_name='validate_kiro_project_path';" # 0

  # Contrast - these DO work:
  sqlite3 $DB "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM refs WHERE reference_name IN ('unwrap','expect');"        # 5361
  sqlite3 $DB "SELECT DISTINCT reference_name FROM refs WHERE reference_name LIKE '%::from';" # PathBuf::from, InstallMode::from, ...

  # Ground truth: validate_kiro_project_path is called in 6+ command files (ripgrep confirms).

Likely cause: the tree-sitter-rust reference extractor captures call_expression whose function is a field_expression (method call) or scoped_identifier (A::b), but not a plain identifier (bare free-fn call). Suggest extending ref extraction to record call_expression with a bare identifier callee, with reference_name = the identifier and kind = 'call'.
acceptance_criteria:
* After indexing a workspace containing a bare free-function call foo() (function defined in-crate, called without qualification), SELECT COUNT(*) FROM refs WHERE reference_name='foo' returns >= 1.
* tethys callers <free_fn> lists the bare-call sites.
* Existing method-call and qualified-call ref extraction is unchanged (regression test).
* A unit/integration fixture pins a bare free-fn call producing a refs row.
notes:
INVALID — closed not-a-bug (2026-06-30, verified against code + live self-index).

The stated root cause is wrong: src/languages/rust.rs:271 has an IDENTIFIER arm in extract_call_reference that emits a Call ref for bare foo() — present since the Phase-2 commit (303f2db), well before this was filed. Bare free-function calls ARE captured, stored, resolved, and present in both refs and call_edges.

The repro measured the wrong column. Resolution NULLs reference_name (src/db/references.rs:157: UPDATE refs SET symbol_id=?2, reference_name=NULL), and initial storage only sets reference_name when symbol_id IS NULL. So reference_name is populated ONLY for UNRESOLVED refs (std method calls like unwrap/expect). make_service resolved in-crate => reference_name NULL => the WHERE reference_name='make_service' query is structurally empty.

Proof (self-index): bare free fn node_text => 0 rows WHERE reference_name='node_text', but 60 refs + 37 call_edges via JOIN symbols on symbol_id. Dead-code finder would NOT flag it.

The genuine issue behind the symptom is the reference_name nulling itself (refs not name-queryable for resolved symbols) — filed separately. The fn-as-value / macro-token gap is tracked in tethys-ygjx.

Closed: Not a bug: bare free-fn calls are captured (verified). Repro queried reference_name, which resolution NULLs for resolved in-crate symbols. Superseded by the reference_name-nulling issue (see notes).

title: Hotspots: rank symbols by connectivity (in+out degree)
description:
Add a hotspots query that ranks symbols by total edge degree (incoming + outgoing) excluding structural edges, surfacing the most-connected code in the codebase. Useful for identifying core abstractions and high-blast-radius change points before refactoring.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_hotspots tool and CLI command. See crates/tethys/KIROGRAPH-COMPARISON.md.

**Implementation:**
- Pure SQL on the existing schema:
  SELECT s.id, COUNT(*) AS degree FROM symbols s
  JOIN (SELECT caller_symbol_id AS sid FROM call_edges
        UNION ALL
        SELECT callee_symbol_id AS sid FROM call_edges
        UNION ALL
        SELECT symbol_id AS sid FROM refs WHERE symbol_id IS NOT NULL
        UNION ALL
        SELECT in_symbol_id AS sid FROM refs WHERE in_symbol_id IS NOT NULL) e
    ON e.sid = s.id
  GROUP BY s.id ORDER BY degree DESC LIMIT ?
- Public API: Tethys::get_hotspots(limit) -> Vec<HotspotEntry { symbol, in_degree, out_degree, total_degree }>
- CLI: tethys hotspots [--limit N] [--json]

## 2026-07-12 pi-lens implementation review — reference scoring models

Two legible, hand-tuned models from pi-lens worth adapting (both deliberately linear and inspectable — no opaque scores):

**Risk flags** (clients/review-graph/query.ts:213-231): per symbol/file — exported-symbol-changed; high fanout (>= 4 outgoing call edges); high complexity (cyclomatic >= 8); boundary-wrapper; cycle-adjacent (an importer that is also imported). Their thresholds are duplicated in two modules (query.ts and module-report.ts:637-639) — centralize ours in one place (their drift is the lesson).

**Recommended-reads ranker** (clients/module-report.ts:751-802): score = refs*2 + complexity + (exported ? 2 : 0) + (high-complexity flag ? 3 : 0) + task-focus term (+6 per task-hint substring hit); top 5 kept. The task-focus term only makes sense on an interactive surface (act 2 / tethys-o4re could use it for a "what to read" MCP tool); the structural part maps to this issue.

**tethys field mapping (verified against schema 2026-07-12):**
- in/out degree: call_edges + refs, both indexed — the core of this issue as titled.
- exportedness: symbols visibility field.
- cycle-adjacency: derivable from the existing cycles analysis (SQL CTE).
- cyclomatic complexity: NOT extracted today — would need a per-symbol extraction step (tree-sitter node counting). Scope decision: v1 = connectivity-only per this issue's title; complexity as a follow-up flag if wanted.
- centrality formula candidate shared with tethys-27kv (FTS5 search): log-damped importer count, 1 + ln(1 + importers)/4 — one implementation, two consumers.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] get_hotspots() public API method on Tethys
- [ ] CLI subcommand 'tethys hotspots' with --limit and --json flags
- [ ] Returns each entry with in_degree, out_degree, total_degree
- [ ] Excludes structural/contains edges (none currently exist, so noop, but documented)
- [ ] Unit tests on a fixture workspace
- [ ] Integration tested on the rivets workspace itself

title: Streaming SQLite writes during indexing
description:
Write to SQLite immediately after parsing each file instead of accumulating data in memory.

**Current behavior:**
- Parse all files, accumulate symbols/references in memory
- Write to SQLite after parsing completes
- Memory usage: O(n) where n = codebase size

**Drift's pattern:**
- Parse file → immediately send to background writer thread
- Writer batches 100 files per transaction
- Memory usage: O(1) constant regardless of codebase size

**Benefits:**
- Handle 10K+ file codebases without OOM
- Progressive results (can query partial index)
- Better crash recovery (data already persisted)

**Implementation:**
```rust
struct BatchWriter {
    sender: Sender<FileBatch>,
    handle: JoinHandle<WriteStats>,
}

impl BatchWriter {
    fn new(db_path: PathBuf, batch_size: usize) -> Self;
    fn send(&self, batch: FileBatch) -> Result<()>;
    fn finish(self) -> Result<WriteStats>;
}
```

This pairs with rayon parallelization - parsing threads send to writer thread.

title: Add gated integration tests for LSP
description:
Add #[ignore] tests that require rust-analyzer installed:

- lsp_resolves_trait_method_call

- lsp_index_increases_resolution_count

Run with: `cargo test --ignored`

title: refs table omits free-function identifiers used as values and macro-token-tree identifiers
description:
The Rust reference extractor produces refs ONLY for call_expression, struct_expression, and type_identifier nodes (src/languages/rust.rs:165-250). Two real categories of free-function/identifier references are therefore never recorded:

1. Function-as-value: a function referenced WITHOUT calling it — passed as a callback or stored (iter.map(node_text), let f = validate;). This is a plain identifier in argument/value position (not a call_expression, not a type_identifier), so no ref is emitted.
2. Macro token-tree identifiers: identifiers (including calls) that appear only inside a macro invocation's token tree are not parsed into call_expression nodes, so references that exist only at a macro call site are missed.

Impact: a function used ONLY as a value (never called via call_expression) has zero inbound call-refs/call_edges, so it is a FALSE POSITIVE for the dead-code finder (tethys-dvsw) and undercounted by hotspots (tethys-7p54) — which is why those depend on this issue.

Scope note: this is NOT the same as bare CALL foo(), which IS captured correctly (see closed tethys-zp2j). And it is distinct from the reference_name-nulling queryability bug (tethys-6rlu). Category 1 (fn-as-value) is the higher-value half; category 2 (macro tokens) is lower-value and higher false-positive — split if needed.

Provenance: 'Missing refs: fn-as-value and macro token-tree identifiers invisible' from the June 2026 indexing-strategy review; never previously filed (an earlier note cited a phantom ID tethys-cfme).
design: tree-sitter-rust: (1) fn-as-value is an identifier node in expression/argument position whose parent is NOT a call_expression callee and NOT a scoped_identifier — needs a textual/symbol-table guard to avoid recording every identifier as a ref. (2) macro identifiers live under token_tree inside macro_invocation; the extractor currently does not descend into token_tree. Both are higher-false-positive than zp2j's bare-call case, so apply conservative guards (suppressions, not accusations) per the project's error posture.
acceptance_criteria:
* fn-as-value: indexing a workspace where an in-crate free function foo is passed as a value (e.g. iter.map(foo) or let g = foo;) yields >= 1 refs row with reference_name='foo' (kind distinguishing value-use from call is acceptable).
* macro-token identifier: an explicit decision is recorded — either a refs row is produced for an in-crate symbol referenced only inside a macro token tree, or it is documented as out-of-scope with rationale.
* Existing bare-call (tethys-zp2j), method-call, and qualified-call extraction is unchanged (regression test).
* Conservative: no spurious refs for shadowed locals / non-symbol identifiers.
notes:
Third concrete instance (found by tethys-jdly design fixture, 2026-07-02): unit-struct constructor in value position — 'crate::OldStruct' as an expression (e.g. return value) emits NO ref (scoped identifier in value position; only the type-position use 'fn f() -> crate::OldStruct' gets a type ref). Same class as category 1. Fixture: .tethys-jdly/ design notes; affects deprecated-callers recall for deprecated unit structs constructed via qualified paths.

Closed: Shipped: PR #19 merged to main (a54ca7b). New 'value' ref kind records fn-as-value (row_to_symbol: 0->13 value refs by symbol_id); per-function scope-guard suppresses shadowed locals (probe: 24 genuine kept / 59 local-shadows suppressed, 0 errors); unresolved value refs dropped after Pass-2; excluded from call_edges (callers/impact unchanged). ACs: (1) fn-as-value fenced by value_ref_emitted_for_fn_as_arg/_let_binding + unresolved_value_ref_dropped (name-queryable via refs_named per 6rlu); (2) macro-tokens documented out-of-scope -> tethys-8ym0, fenced by macro_token_identifier_not_emitted_as_value; (3) existing extraction unchanged -> existing_ref_kinds_unchanged; (4) conservative -> value_ref_suppressed_for_shadowed_local. Fixed in-branch: parse_reference_kind was a duplicate kind-parser (broke on 'value'), collapsed to delegate to ReferenceKind::parse. Filed: tethys-8ym0 (macro tokens), tethys-i09d (scoped-path values), tethys-wbrh (extend value positions), tethys-c3kj (block-precise suppression). y3bx re-pointed to depend on 8ym0.

title: nightly workflow: LSP ignored tests + criterion bench run-and-record
description:
The 11 LSP integration tests (tests/lsp_*.rs) are all #[ignore]d and run NOWHERE automatically; criterion benches never run in CI (verified 2026-07-12). Ignored tests rot against API drift — pi-lens's nightly live-smoke lesson (grill session 2026-07-12).

Decided shape — one scheduled (cron) workflow, two jobs:
1. lsp-smoke: install rust-analyzer on the runner, run the ignored tests (cargo nextest run --run-ignored ...) as real pass/fail; failure notifies via GitHub's workflow-failure email.
2. bench-record: run criterion benches to completion, upload timings as an artifact for eyeball trends. NO automated regression thresholds — shared-runner noise would train the maintainer to ignore red (same failure mode as fuzzy indeterminate triggers, see the affected-tests standing issue).

Not per-PR: keeps the PR gate fast; nightly is the right cadence for environment-dependent smoke.

title: Implement proper staleness check
description:
Currently `needs_update()` in `lib.rs:2108-2110` always returns `true`. Should check if any indexed files have changed by comparing stored `mtime_ns`/`size_bytes` against filesystem.

Related to incremental updates but useful independently for skip-if-fresh optimization.

title: Architecture analysis: package detection and coupling metrics (Ca/Ce/instability)
description:
Detect high-level project structure (packages and architectural layers) and compute coupling metrics between them. Useful for refactor planning, codebase health monitoring, and PR review.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's architecture/coupling/package commands. The metrics (afferent coupling, efferent coupling, instability) are Robert C. Martin classics — well-established and immediately legible to seasoned engineers.

**What we already have:**
- Cargo.toml discovery via cargo::discover_crates → CrateInfo with name + path.
- file_deps table with from_file_id → to_file_id edges.
- Both feed naturally into per-crate rollups; no new parsing required.

**What this adds:**
- arch_packages table: id, name, path, source ('manifest'|'directory'), language, version, manifest_path.
- arch_file_packages: file_id → package_id mapping.
- arch_package_deps: package_id → package_id with import_count, rolled up from file_deps.
- arch_coupling: per-package Ca, Ce, instability = Ce / (Ca + Ce).

Definitions:
- Ca (afferent coupling): how many other packages depend on this one. Higher = more stable / load-bearing.
- Ce (efferent coupling): how many packages this one depends on. Higher = more unstable.
- Instability: 0 = maximally stable (everyone depends on it, it depends on nothing); 1 = maximally unstable.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::get_packages() -> Vec<Package>
- Tethys::get_coupling_metrics(package_name: Option<&str>) -> Vec<CouplingEntry>
- Tethys::get_package_dependencies(package_id) -> Vec<PackageDep>

**CLI:**
- tethys architecture [--format table|json]
- tethys coupling [--package NAME] [--sort instability|ca|ce|name]
- tethys package <NAME> [--no-files]

**Layer detection (lower priority, follow-up):**
KiroGraph also classifies files into layers (api/service/data/ui/shared) by path patterns. Could be a v2; the package-level metrics are the high-value primary feature.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Schema migration adds arch_packages, arch_file_packages, arch_package_deps, arch_coupling
- [ ] Architecture analysis runs as a phase during indexing (only when enabled — opt-in flag)
- [ ] CLI: tethys architecture, tethys coupling, tethys package
- [ ] Coupling metrics correctly compute Ca, Ce, instability for the rivets workspace itself
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_coupling and tethys_architecture (sibling rivets-o4re)
- [ ] Documented in tethys README
- [ ] Tests verify metrics on a multi-crate fixture workspace

title: Per-language display spelling for qualified names at query/output boundary
description: tethys stores qualified names with a canonical `::` separator for all languages (a cache format, decided in .separator-fix/spec.md decision #5). C# consumers see `AuthService::Login` in CLI/library output where C# syntax would be `AuthService.Login`. Add a display-formatting layer at the query/output boundary that re-spells qualified names per the symbol's language. Storage stays canonical `::`; this is presentation only. Deferred from the separator-fix loop (see .separator-fix/spec.md, probe findings in .separator-fix/probe-findings.md).

title: Attributes on pub-use re-exports are unrepresentable: #[deprecated] alias renames invisible to analyses
description:
Found by the tethys-jdly prove-it probe against vendored zbus 4.4.0 (amazon-q-developer-cli).

**Symptom**: 32 of zbus's 44 `#[deprecated]` attributes sit on `pub use` re-exports (the alias-rename idiom: `#[deprecated(since=..., note=...)] pub use message::Builder as MessageBuilder;`). None appear in the `attributes` table, so deprecated-callers (tethys-jdly) cannot report uses of deprecated aliases.

**Root cause**: `attributes.symbol_id` references `symbols`; `pub use` produces reexport *refs* (tethys-v1w8), not symbols — there is nowhere to attach the attribute. `extract_preceding_attributes` is only invoked from symbol extraction sites in src/languages/rust.rs.

**Design tension**: attaching attributes to refs needs either a nullable second FK (attributes.ref_id) or a reexport pseudo-symbol; note resolution semantics differ (the deprecation is on the alias *path*, not the target symbol — a caller using the target's canonical path is NOT deprecated).

**Scope note**: tethys-jdly ships without this; documented as a known limitation there. Probe artifacts: .tethys-jdly/findings.md.

title: Adopt rstest for parameterized tests in tethys
description: PR review recommends adopting rstest for parameterized tests per CLAUDE.md guidelines. Current tests are well-structured but many could be consolidated with rstest case attributes. Low priority - functional tests already exist.

title: Rust extractor: single-segment glob imports (use crate_name::*) store empty source_module
description: parse_use_wildcard (src/languages/rust.rs) walks use_wildcard children looking only for SCOPED_IDENTIFIER; a root-level glob's path is a bare identifier/crate/self/super node, so path collects empty and the imports row lands as symbol_name='*', source_module=''. Multi-segment globs (use a::b::*) are unaffected. Blinds any consumer keying imports by module path: unused-imports corroboration, file-deps, and the xoxq visibility-tightening glob evidence channel. Discovered by the xoxq S2 stress fixture (RED run).
notes: Closed: Fixed in feat/tethys-xoxq-visibility-tightening (parse_use_wildcard matches identifier/crate/self/super path children; unit fence extracts_single_segment_glob_use)

title: C# namespace resolution for using statements
description:
`resolve_import()` in `csharp.rs:109-111` returns empty vec, meaning C# `using` statements don't resolve to files.

This was marked "Task 6" in the original TODO, suggesting it was planned. Need to implement namespace-to-file resolution for C# projects.
notes: Closed by the csharp-ns loop (branch csharp-ns): CSharpModuleResolver resolves plain using-directives via the namespace map (types-only, unique-or-decline); the resolve_csharp_dependencies post-pass is deleted with C# file deps derived from resolved refs under L2 semantics. Audit trail in .csharp-ns/.
